There's a lot to digest in this book, and the twelve basic concepts are fairly simple, really. But the author spends so much time circling some of the focal points that I found myself losing track of what, precisely, he was trying to say.

A little background: I was raised in a predominantly Methodist household, "dropped out" of all things religious for many years, and got back into it fairly late in life. After I retired in 2003, I returned to college at a small Wesleyan university to major in music and ended up minoring in theology. I was also a church musician for over 20 years, in both United Methodist as well as Episcopal churches. So I have very strong "Wesleyan" roots, and it is from that perspective that I approached this book.

The biggest problem I have is the use of overly complicated explanations to illustrate the points the author is trying to make.

The first chapter is a good example. "Worship, not Performance: How We Love God". This chapter seems to be encouraging us to avoid the appearance of acting like a "proper" Christian, "performing" the role, and instead to engage in unselfconscious enthusiastic worship. How it gets there is a long winding road.

The opening example is a description of how the author's children naturally responded to praise band music by dancing in the aisles to illustrate "uninhibited worship." From the first part of the chapter: "That is worship. That's what human existence is supposed to be. God is the DJ of the dance party that is our world. Like every good DJ, God's goal is to make us dance with abandon and wonder. Without an agenda. Without worrying what other people think."

We then journey through Adam and Eve's loss of innocence and the birth of self-consciousness that resulted from eating the fruit; through the forest of self-justification and it's relationship to hypocrisy (using examples from the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 6:2-6, 16-18). Per the author: "Religion has always been filled with divas trying to outperform each other. If you're really good at being right, it's to your advantage to make worshiping God into an übercompetitive performance of posturing with all the right pieties. This is how Christians turn the worship that is supposed to cultivate innocent delight in God into a masquerade ball of parroted right answers and exhibitionist false humility."

OK, I get it, I think. But the author also seems to imply that unless we dance in the aisles, we're not doing "authentic worship," that the only legitimate response to casting off the yoke of self-justification / pious "performance" is to adopt the child's response born out of innocence -- "Faith isn't the performance that passes God's test to earn us a ticket to heaven; it's the abolition of performance that liberates us from the hell of self-justification and restores us to a life of authentic worship." I would counter: cannot "authentic worship" include allowing a hymn (old or new) to transport us to a quiet but intense understanding of God's presence? "Letting oneself go" in the presence of God doesn't necessarily have to involve the same actions/behaviors for every person, does it?

I think the underlying concepts here are good ones and clearly belie a comforting Wesleyan "slant." Blind adherence to a particular interpretation of "rules" in an attempt to "earn" one's salvation, with no attempt to consider other alternate interpretations, leads to some pretty ugly behaviors among folks who consider themselves "Christians." Love conquers hate. But I feel they're often overly complicated with a lot of extraneous "stuff." Sometimes, this "scenic route" approach to making the point works (Chapter 5: Honor, Not Terror: How We Live By Truth" is especially good); sometimes, not so much.

Overall, then, there are some good bits here, but it requires a lot of digging sometimes to get there. And some of the side trips around various anecdotes and literature references don't seem to fit the concept very comfortably. Solid 3 stars ("It's OK").

When I was in the second grade (60 years ago), we read a book about a brother and sister in Lapland. I was fascinated by it -- it was my first introduction to the fact that children in other parts of the world lead very different lives to the one I was living in a semi-rural suburb of Kansas City, MO. I vowed then and there that ** someday ** I would visit Lapland and see it for myself.

At that point, I started making a list of "places to see before I die" -- with Lapland as #1 on the list. I kept that list over the years, adding to it year by year as I studied geography, history, art, and music -- all pointing to places that, once I had the time and the means, I wanted to visit. I managed to visit a few of them on business trips, but barely scratched the surface.

I started saving when I was in my 20s so that when the day came that I had the time, I would also have the means to visit those places.

I'm now retired, and I've started working on my list, managing to visit about 1/3 of the entries

I was thinking recently about the book that started it all and how nice it would be to own a copy of it. I figured it was out of print, and I couldn't remember the name of it. Enter Google. I was able to locate it in about 30 seconds and to my delight, I found that it had been reprinted in 2012.

Beautifully illustrated, this would be a wonderful introduction to another culture and time. Each chapter highlights a different aspect of life for children living in the far north of Scandinavia -- the northern parts of Norway and Finland. The characters -- including the animals -- are beautifully drawn and you really end up feeling like you know these children.

So I'm rereading the account of Lise and Lasse, this time through adult eyes eager to reconnect with old "friends." And if things go according to plan, I'll actually visit the land of the Northlights in the not-too-distant future.
Numbers way too small for these old eyes.

I wasn't sure what to expect when I selected this book to review, but what I got was a very nice collection of recipes that are meant to represent the dishes and treats mentioned in the Harry Potter series.

Each recipe includes a specific reference to the book and chapter. Since this is an unofficial cookbook, the recipe titles themselves don't always match the names used in the books, but one can get a sense of connection from the descriptions of the dishes.

The book is designed to give the appearance of an older book: page edges are untrimmed, the paper includes "shading" to give the appearance of parchment, and the ink is sepia, rather than black. There are NO illustrations at all, but there are some interesting "sidebars." The recipes themselves are fairly standard British-food type recipes, recipes that you might find in any good British cookbook. They've been "edited" for U.S. cooks in terms of measurements and ingredients, and most appear to be reasonably easy to prepare, although some of the instructions seem overly complex.

Of the recipes I've made (probably 6 or 8 in all), my favorites are "Herby Roast Chicken with Onion-Garlic Mashed Potatoes" (from "Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix," Chapter 9) and "Rhubard Crumble with Custard Sauce" (from "Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix," Chapter 5). These are good solid recipes that produce good results.

The problem is, I have several good British cookbooks that contain similar recipes, and that also include illustrations. The only thing this book has over those is the unofficial Harry Potter connection.

This book is a cute novelty item for die-hard Harry Potter fans, and might even serve to introduce a whole new generation to British food (it's not ALL over cooked soggy peas and non-descript meat). The recipes seem to be good representatives of that country's cuisine. But if you already have a couple of good British cookbooks in your collection, and you don't really care about the Harry Potter connection, you probably won't find much new here.
It is a very difficult concept

There's a lot to like here. I found it to be a very comprehensive guide to combining both colors and different media to get some really interesting effects.

I've been coloring since the 1980s -- back when it really wasn't "fashionable" for adults to color. I've done a couple of classes in colored pencils and alcohol markers. Reinert covers both of those in detail, plus gel pens, pastels, and water-soluble tools (think watercolors as well as watercolor pencils) individually as well as how to use them in combinations (gel pens over colored pencils, for example).

I found it very helpful in both reinforcing the techniques I already knew plus introducing techniques that were new to me. I think this book works best if you've had a little experience coloring some of the more detailed drawings that are so popular today.

Bottom line: I really like it, and I'll definitely use it to expand my own technical "toolbox." Solid 4 stars.
Short...And Pimmsleur was better!

I have very mixed feeling about this book. On the one hand, it's beautifully produced, typical of other Dorling Kindersley (DK) publishers. The photos are clear, the construction is first-rate (the cover especially). It has a substantial feel about it.

However, the content is another matter. I do papercrafts -- mostly hand-crafted greeting cards, some quilling, 3D decoupage, and a smattering of origami. This book contains examples of most of those (no decoupage), but with very limited projects. You would think from looking at the cover (and seeing all the "Sponsored products related to this item" that Amazon so conveniently exposes on the product page) that quilling would be somewhat featured in the book. Not so. Only a few pages are devoted to quilling and the primary project featured is a pair of earrings which although nice aren't that useful, really.

My primary craft, card making, is also covered only minimally, and what's there isn't again very useful or unique.

Where the book shines, however, is the section on making large over-sized flowers out of sheets of crepe paper. Almost 1/3 of the book is devoted to this, and the results are stunning. Useful? maybe, maybe not. You'd expect with the number of pages and projects illustrating this craft that there would be some sponsored product ads related to the supplies needed to produce these (crepe paper sheets or rolls, etc.), but no.

So if you're looking for some sort of introductory book that illustrates a variety of paper crafts in enough depth to actually produce something useful, you might be disappointed. If you're looking for a book that is primarily about making large (albeit beautiful) crepe paper flowers, with a smattering of info about some other paper crafts, this might be just the book for you. I can give it no more than 3 stars (it's OK).

I'm old enough to remember the 1964 earthquake that hit coastal Alaska, seeing grainy television film of Anchorage in ruins. But it was so far away from my home in Kansas City that I promptly lost track (and interest) in the aftermath.

It was when I moved to northern California and experienced my first earthquakes that I got more interested in earthquakes in general. Henry Fountain has given us a compelling and dramatic account of the Alaska quake that provides just enough science for a layperson to grasp but at the same time explaining the significance of this event.

One of the surprises for me was the tsunamis that followed the quake. I only vaguely remember the flooding but it was massive -- and resulted in the bulk of the 130 fatalities.

Fountain has been compared to Eric Larson. There are a lot of similarities -- especially the focus on real people and how they reacted to or coped with the disaster unfolding, and the chronological focus that helps keep the reader interested in "what happened next?" Another similarity: I've read most of Larson's books multiple times, and I'll absolutely read this again -- I'm taking it along on a cruise vacation to Alaska next week. It's the kind of book that opens up new details with each reading, things I might have passed over in previous readings.

Bottom line: I really liked this.

This is another addict's story; I can't say that it is memorable in any particular way except that it is about a gay man.  The issues he deals with are not particular to gay men and so anyone interested in what low bottom addiction is really about can find out by reading this book.

First things first: This is NOT a detailed travel guide. It is an introduction to what the editors believe are the "10 best countries, 10 best regions, 10 best cities, and 10 best values" in travel for 2019. It serves as a guide to open up deeper exploration. I believe it succeeds beautifully in this respect.

I've only recently started traveling more -- 3-4 times a year -- trying to whittle down my list of "places I must visit before I die." I'm using this book (and I presume future editions that will follow) to start my more detailed research on specific areas that might be on the agenda for this year's travel plans.

The book gives just enough information to give you an idea as to WHY it was selected on the relevant "10 best ... " list. If I find a place interesting, I start doing more research on that place to see 1) how to get there 2) where I might stay 3) what sights I might want to examine while there. But this book opens the door for me and gets me interested.

It's also a great "armchair traveler" book for those that can't make the actual trips to places of interest. Again, there's enough info to give you an idea of why the editors considered a particular place "special" enough to include, and from there, you can pursue more detailed study.

I like this book a lot -- and will continue to use it and any subsequent editions to help shape my future travels. I've used Lonely Planet travel guides for years -- this is a nice addition to the library. Solid 5 stars from me.
Absolutely couldn't stand the way it was written, pretty much unreadable

I've looked at a few scriptural / Biblical coloring books and really haven't found one that "speaks" to me -- most are either too literal, or the image appears to have no connection to the scripture it's supposed to represent.

This lovely book has neither of those issues. The illustrations are clearly connected to the accompanying scripture but in a nice sometimes subtle way. For example, a butterfly design is used to illustrate 2 Corinthians 5:17 ("Therefore if any man be in Christ, he is a new creation ... ").

These are nice illustrations, too. There is plenty of variety -- mandalas, nature scenes, geometric patterns, florals -- some with incredible detail, others with more wide open spaces, plus several with a combination of the two.

Another nice touch: the artist, Pamela Thayer, has included in each illustration a tiny cross. Part of the fun of coloring each page is finding that little cross (and I do mean little!) and highlighting it in some way. I've included a couple of shots of a page I'm working on right now so you can see how one of these is included in the image.

I really do like this book. So why 4 stars? Some of the designs are printed so close to the binding that it's very difficult to color them. There's no easy way to get the pages out of the book, either (no perforation/etc.). They ARE printed on one side only, so that's a major plus. I usually use either alcohol markers or colored pencils, and both work fine with the paper. It's not terribly thick, though, and alcohol marker bleeds through, so you'll want to place a couple of sheets of paper under your image to prevent colors from bleeding onto the next page.

Overall, nice book nice use of scripture as inspiration for illustration; wish it were easier to remove pages to color near the edge.​

My niece recommended this book. She loved it. I'd heard of it, but never seen it.  It's just not for me. I find the layout very confusing. It's very hard to read. Another person may side with my niece. But this book is not for me.

I want to like it. I give great berth to many styles of writing. The overall movement and lack of depth makes this a summertime or night time read. Just fluff. But... FFS. Is a teen age boy writing this? Someone who is jotting out their fantasies? It's tiring, and overdone. I want a good story where I come up with my own images, which I am left to do with the males of this story. But the females? I am given every detail. It doesn't make me uncomfortable, it makes me cringe. I trudged through because I was curious "who done it". Some male writers can write very interesting, complex women, and then there is this. So many writers out there, and this is what I ended up buying? For shame. I am not really liking the main character either. I am really just trudging through it for the mystery. Not sure if I will stay because of how the characters are handled, so far. We'll see. I'll be back to update if I have some major change of heart.

Now if weak characters (and minimal/no development) is your thing, then have at it. This review is opinion, and we are free to read what we like. I am just trying to help those looking to avoid total "females as objects" handling.

I am rather on board with the story, but every other line has some reference to how a female looks. It is all about her tits, or her ass, or juicy lips, perfection/hotness or how she is giving erections to every guy, and how every male is all about looking the female/s up and down because they are incapable of doing anything else. The female voices are either over the top sexualized, or irrational/crazy/hysterical. Pretty sure I just got a glimpse into this writers mind, hm? Just over and over and over..and over. The females are objects. So terrible it is laughable, if it weren't so sad. And the the main character? just "rugged". Ex baseball player. Owns a bikini laden bar and he is all about hook ups, and cheated on his wife, who he only depicts as crazy. And it is all so.. cliched. All "tell". Good writers can get some points across without bashing the reader over the head with it. This writer is like, "look what I can do!". One hand on the keyboard, and one hand.. well, you know.

Maybe, just maybe the main character is being treated as a frat boy and he eventually evolves or something, but as a reader, I am not seeing it as the same perception is applied to other male characters and how they look & perceive the female characters.

Guh, I hate that I spend money on this. Chose it because I like this genre, seems like it could have been a fun read. I love authors that use one character over several books. I saw that this novel was a good length (not too short) and had several books with this character. If you can get past the extremely weak female characters, then this might be just the story for you. Think I will spend my money elsewhere.

There are parts of this book that are laugh-out-loud side-splitting funny; others not so much. But taken as a whole, this is one entertaining read.

Celia Rivenbark is at her best when she's describing people and their idiosyncracies. She has a special knack for creating characters that you feel you've met somewhere, except her characters take on larger-than-life personae.

For me, the first section "On the House," about her remodeling experiences, is the least successful. It's amusing, but I've seen it/read it all before. The rest of the book, however, is fresh and very very funny. Part III - It's Raining Men is hysterical!

This is the kind of book you can pick up and put down -- the chapters are short -- which makes it the perfect book for bathroom reading, taking along on doctor appointments, commuting.

If you're looking for something light that can be taken in small doses, Belle Weather just might be a good fit.

What a treat! I've been collecting pop-up books for 15 years, and Matthew Reinhart is one of my favorite paper engineers. This is a wonderful example of his style: highly detailed, lots of opportunities for interaction (things to pull, spin, open, etc.).

Along with all the wonderfully ingenious paper crafting, you get a fascinating history of the Lego brand and a closer look at some of their more popular lines. Some of the pop-ups are "transformer"-like -- you lift a panel, then pull a tab to transform it into something completely different. Very clever, indeed.

As with most of these complex pop-up creations, a gentle hand is necessary especially when brand new. I found a couple of places where a bit was a bit "stuck" -- just needed a gentle nudge to move freely.

This would make a great gift for any pop-up book aficionado OR a Lego fan. I loved it!
I would not have bought if knew cults were included too. Christ-mas ones cute.

Beautiful, but very, very thin with no padding.
I made a little inner bag from quilted cotton batting for it, as the corners of my deck distressed the fabric & would eventually ruin it. Felt would also work.
It is visually gorgeous & matches the deck precisely.

This book is an advertisement for a coaching program that costs $1000.

DO NOT waste your money. The information provided is very general and will not help you.

This will be useful for the most part, but it needs some serious editing. It appears to have been pieced together from a variety of sources, at least one of which is British ("solicitor" instead of "attorney" or "lawyer," "neighbours," etc.). Also, there's a glaring misspelling on one of the pages ("Ccountant" instead of "Accountant"). A good editor might have caught these.

As I said, it will be somewhat useful -- but I think there might be better versions of the same idea available. 3 stars: it's OK. (Still hoping for a digital version of one of these instead of having to handwrite all the entries.)

This is a very nice collection of doily patterns by some of the "big names": Delsie Rhoades, Dot Drake, Patricia Kristoffersen, Lucille LaFlamme, C. Strohmeyer. The patterns feature the "pineapple" design, an old favorite due to its ease to create and ability to fit into a lot of different decorating styles.

It's a small book, about 5 1/2" x 8 1/2" and 34 pages. But you have 6 really lovely patterns, all of them appealing.

Five of the patterns call for size 10 thread; one calls for size 20. I'm of an age where seeing smaller thread can be an issue. I've never let that stop me, however. I've had no problems substituting larger thread in these patterns calling for smaller thread. I just use size 10 thread with a size 1.5mm hook. Yes, the doily ends up larger, but I'm OK with that. So if you like a pattern that calls for smaller thread than you're comfortable using, never be afraid to work it with larger thread. With the right size hook, it should come out just fine.

All that aside, the patterns are super, easy to follow for an intermediate crocheter (I wouldn't recommend these for a beginner -- you really do need some experience reading patterns in order to fell comfortable with these).

Book arrived with a piece of sticky candy stuck on all the edges of the bottom of the book. Had to cut the largest amount off with a sharp knife and then remove the rest with a damp cloth. The damp cloth caused the pages to warp. When most of the candy was removed and the book was carefully opened one of the pages had a piece turn out because the candy had leaked in between the pages.

Not quite as good as some other DK travel guides I've used in the past, but good for an intro to travel in Italy.  There's probably not enough detail for those who want a complete guide, but it's rare that a single book will provide all the info you really need to explore an area.  I'll be cruising around Italy for a couple of weeks in early 2020, and this provides some basic info for some of the ports, but not all of them.  I'll definitely need another book to "fill in the blanks."  That said, the photos are helpful as are the maps.  I might even suggest starting with this book to get an overview of travel in Italy, then zero in on another book or three for more detail (I like Fodors, Lonely Planet, and Rick Steves).  I like this as a solid intro guide -- four stars.

There are so many things I didn’t like about this book. I’ve no patience for, nor interest in reading books about,  women who are doormats - especially with her fiancé. He’s never around - she doesn’t know why or where he is - and she tolerates that. The majority of the characters are unpleasant and unlikable. Georgie wasn’t this stupid in the previous book. I stopped counting how many times she said, “Oh golly.”

This review is for the KINDLE edition of this book. I have both the paperback as well as the Kindle versions. The paperback version rates 5 stars. However, the Kindle version has some serious formatting issues that really interfere with its usability.

The patterns here are reprinted from old crochet pattern leaflets, and the instructions are reasonably clear and easy enough to follow for intermediate- to advance-level crocheters. And there is a black-and-white photo of each project, including many "close-up" views of the individual motifs.

The problem arises when the photo(s) of a project are separated from the instructions. For example, you see a photo of "Hospitality Tablecloth" - looks wonderful! I want to do this one! Under the photo: "Directions on page 72" -- uh, OK. Where is page 72? There aren't any page numbers in the Kindle edition. So you have to flip through page after page of other photos and instructions until you find the set of instructions under a DIFFERENT photo for another tablecloth (whose directions are somewhere else) that are labeled for the photo of the item you wish to make -- in this case, ** 20 ** pages after the photo. How difficult would it have been for someone to either include clickable links to the instruction page or actually move things around so that the photos are followed immediately by the directions?

I love Dover's needlecraft series, and they've done a lot to make these vintage patterns available for a reasonable price. But if you're going to do a Kindle edition of something, I believe that a little care should be taken to reformat to fit the idiosyncrasies of that device (i.e. "locations" rather than "page numbers").

The patterns are still useful, the photos are still relatively clear, but finding what you're looking for is frankly a royal pain. I'm giving this 3 stars -- it's OK. I don't hate it, but it's more difficult to use than it should be.

I got this for my mother. I assumed as she feels this way, she would enjoy reading the book. She did not. She said it was very poorly written and was very hard to follow. Yes, she is 84 but her mind is sharp and she knows what she likes. If she did not like this, it must not have been well written at all. She subscribes to what the authors were trying to convey....she just said it was written as though it was a book to make money, not make America understand what is happening.

I asked her how many stars to give it and she said none. I can't do that so it gets 1.

This arrived just in time for the Apple Bowl here in Washington state -- the annual matchup between the University of Washington Huskies and the Washington State Cougars that is played the day after Thanksgiving. I quickly paged through it and immediately found three recipes that would work nicely for the viewing party at a friend's house.

Before getting into the details of the recipes, a few words about the organization, appearance, and usability. The book is organized nicely with typical cookbook chapters: Starters; Soups, Salads & Flatbreads; Sandwiches & Burgers; Barbecue & Other Mains; Sides; Desserts; Drinks; and Basics. Although not every recipe has a photo, it looks like about half do, and they're good quality. The author is the creator of The Hungry Fan website and the recipes are from that site. About 1/3 or so have been contributed by sports "stars" -- Andre Agassi, Lebron James for example. The text is fairly easy to read, and color is used to highlight tips/tricks, suggestions, and background material. Overall, an attractive book that's easy to read.

The index is not terribly comprehensive, however, and that's my first "gripe." Since I was preparing party food for the annual Apple Cup game, I was interested in having at least one recipe that featured apples. Go to the index -- "apples" is nowhere to be found. Hmmmm .... doesn't seem likely that there isn't a single recipe that uses apples. Did a manual search (flipping page by page) and found “Team USA Fruit Salad” with apples (along with strawberries, blueberries, blackberries, and watermelon). OK, so maybe it’s not listed because as far as I can tell only one recipe in the book uses apples. Let’s look at another Pacific Northwest staple: salmon. Back to the Index — and “Salmon” isn’t listed either. That’s really strange — since there are 3 recipes that feature salmon. A couple of those recipes even include Salmon in the title, so maybe they’re not in the index because they’re listed in the Table of Contents. Back to the front of the book and … hmmm … no real Table of Contents, either; just the main categories with starting page numbers are listed; no individual recipes by name. Going to the first page of each category doesn’t help, either, since the individual recipes aren’t listed there.

So navigation and trying to find recipes using specific ingredients might be a problem.

Recipe accuracy might be somewhat questionable, too. I always carefully read at least 1/2 of the recipes in EVERY cookbook before reviewing it, just to be sure there are no glaring errors or issues. Unfortunately, I found one here. In the recipe for Dijon Mustard (page 246), the description in red at the top of the page says “If you’re aiming for a honey Dijon, omit the sugar entirely in this recipe and use 3 tablespoons of honey.” Great! I love honey-mustard dressing. Looking at the ingredients, however, there is NO SUGAR listed. None. There is this, however: “2 tablespoons raw honey” in the ingredients list. Sooooo … do I just increase that to 3 tablespoons of raw honey? or do I omit the 2 tablespoons of raw honey and use 3 tablespoons of "regular" honey instead? or is the recipe already set up to be honey mustard? And how many other recipes have similar problems? This tells me I need to proceed with caution when preparing the recipes here.

OK, so how good are the recipes? I prepared 3 for my party from this book. I didn’t spot any ingredient issues as mentioned above, and made them as written:

1. Bacon Chicken Salad Mini Open-Faced Sammies.
This is an easy chicken salad recipe with chicken, bacon, mayo/mustard, smoked paprika, and tarragon, served on a toasted slice of ciabatta, topped with a slice of cheese and placed under the broiler just until the cheese melts. Good stuff. I made the salad at home, then assembled the chicken “sandwiches” on site. May do this again at home, as the salad should keep in the fridge for a couple of days at least.

2. New York-Style Smoked Salmon Sandwich
Since I had a lot of ciabatta bread, this was a no-brainer for the party. I used some local Lummi Island smoked salmon. This is a riff on lox & bagels, but served on ciabatta with fresh baby spinach, onion (I used our sweet Walla Walla onions), and tomato, with cream cheese and capers. Nice twist on a NYC classic.

3. Guinness Cupcakes with Bailey’s Frosting
I really liked these. A good basic chocolate cupcake with the addition of some dark brown goodness (Guinness Extra Stout), topped with a classic buttercream icing made with Bailey’s Irish Cream. This one is the clear winner of the three recipes. Will absolutely make this one again.

So there it is. There are some interesting twists on classic “tailgate” food here, and some really creative recipes. Navigation might be tricky since there’s no comprehensive Table of Contents or Index, and at least one recipe contains a clear error. So I like this — 4 stars for creativity.

Grace Ring, the designer of this book, has stolen the show. It's visually captivating. Unfortunately, the text by Lisa Lenard-Cook is not as original.

If you are one of Ms Lenard-Cook's students, or a member of her community at the Writer's Digest site, you will certainly want to own this summary of her teachings. If, however, you have taken classes elsewhere, attended conferences, or have read other books about writing, you will find nothing new here. It isn't that her advice isn't sound, because it is. It's just that it's overly familiar.

Some may say that so much has been written and said about the subject that nothing more can be added, but this is not true. Story theorists, discouraged by the inability of so many contemporary stories to engage readers, have investigated epics and other classic tales to unravel their secrets.

If you are a beginning female writer, "The Mind of Your Story" might be a good place to start (though, be warned, the author's self-consciously literary style and transparent promotion of her own work can be a bit off-putting). If you have had any experience at all, you would be better off with one of the following books:

[[ASIN:0060391685 Story: Substance, Structure, Style and The Principles of Screenwriting]]
[[ASIN:1932907114 Stealing Fire from the Gods: The Complete Guide to Story for Writers and Filmmakers (2nd Edition)]]
[[ASIN:193290736X The Writers Journey: Mythic Structure for Writers, 3rd Edition]]
[[ASIN:1436709210 The Art Of Dramatic Writing: Its Basis In The Creative Interpretation Of Human Motives]]
[[ASIN:0385480016 Bird by Bird: Some Instructions on Writing and Life]]
[[ASIN:0060919884 The Writing Life]]
[[ASIN:0743455967 On Writing]]
For a top-notch, practical guide to creativity, try
[[ASIN:158542630X The Complete Artist's Way: Creativity as a Spiritual Practice]]

Nice collection of crocheted snowflake patterns. Good variety, not difficult at all. The patterns use chain, slip stitch, single, half double, and double crochet stitches, with a few trebles in some of the patterns. Should be easy for a crocheter with a little experience (advanced beginner, perhaps).

But the BEST thing about this collection: It includes both "text" instructions as well as SYMBOL patterns. If you've ever wanted to learn how to read a symbol crochet pattern, this would be an excellent start as you have both types of instructions for each pattern.

Another major plus is the formatting. Often, Kindle editions can be difficult to follow due to formatting issues. This one is just about perfect. Each set of written instructions appears first (usually 1 page, occasionally 2 pages), followed on the next page by a photo of the snowflake, and on the following page is the symbol pattern almost precisely matching the photo in size and orientation. You can see the written instructions, match those to the photo and match them to the symbol diagram. As I said, just about perfect.

I'll be making a bunch of these this year using metallic crochet threads (I usually work with size 10).

Rituals infuse routine days with meaning and connect us to something larger. Therefore, they are worth observing and celebrating, and food is a large part of that celebration.

So says the author, Jenny Rosenstrach, author of “Dinner: The Playbook” and “Dinner: A Love Story.”
Using getting her children off to school at the bus stop as an example, she presents a series of rituals — some based on holidays like Christmas, Easter, Thanksgiving; some on “situations” like “Lunch with Dad,” “The Sleepover Breakfast,” “Sunday Dinners” — some with anecdotes from her own experience with her children and some that also include recipes and menus that work for those events. The focus is on establishing traditions that can be carried on, not just as a matter of “principle” — “In my family, we always did xxx … xxx, so I have to do it, too” — but because they mean something and help to connect us.

In approaching this book, you need to understand that it’s not “just” a cookbook. Yes, there are recipes, and my testing indicates they're solid. But there’s a lot of narrative, too, explaining the author’s take on a particular ritual, why it’s a ritual, how it became a ritual for her family, etc. She’s a good writer and draws you into her family so you almost feel like you could have lived next door. But if you’re looking for just a lot of recipes and menus for various holidays and events, you might not find this book to your liking.

Since this isn’t “just” a cookbook, I decided to look at two rituals and the associated recipes. My usual practice in reviewing cookbooks is to prepare three recipes just to assess ease of preparation as well as ingredients and flavors.

From the section on “Family Rituals,” I chose “Vacation Rituals”; and from the section “Holidays We Didn’t Invent,” I chose “Fourth of July, aka Cousinland.”

Vacation Rituals —
When you’re a foodie and you’re on vacation, you naturally gravitate to food experiences. You might choose to book a room in a hotel and explore local restaurants, eating “out” every meal. But when you are dealing with traveling with children, you might be better off booking a cottage or condo with a kitchen and cooking “at home” at least some of the time. This gives you an opportunity to explore the local food scene from a different angle: farmers markets, local shops, and bakeries. And thus are vacation rituals born.

So I tried “Grilled Fish Sandwiches with Salsa Fresca.” I live in the Pacific NW and it’s now summer so our farmers market is active. We also have a local fishing cooperative that sells amazing salmon, halibut, and black cod caught by local Lummi Tribal fishers. I used halibut for this recipe.
I made half the recipe — I’m single and didn’t want to have too much in the way of leftovers. The full recipe makes four sandwiches, so I grilled enough fish for 2, assembled one sandwich, reserving the second piece of fish for a repeat a few days later. The marinade has a distinctly Asian slant, with soy sauce and dark sesame oil, that worked well with my halibut. After marinating, you squeeze lime juice over the fish and then quickly grill until just done.
The Salsa Fresca starts with a base of fresh tomatoes chopped with some red onion and cilantro, a splash of red wine vinegar, some olive oil, and whatever other “add-ins” you want: chopped bell peppers, fruit (peaches, pineapple), corn, etc. I added pineapple and some finely chopped jalapeño pepper to counter the sweetness. The final component is simple, but it’s so good I’ll likely use it on other things: mayonnaise mixed with a little Sriracha. I happened to have some ciabatta rolls on hand and used those instead of the baguette recommended in the recipe.
Verdict: a winner, and not too difficult or tricky to prepare. No unusual or difficult to obtain components — you can use any firm fish, and the salsa fresca possibilities are endless, allowing you to take advantage of some local flavors.

Fourth of July —
Rosenstrach sticks with the basics here: chicken, slaw and potato salad. I made the Picnic Chicken and her German Potato Salad
Picnic Chicken —
This is NOT your mother’s “fried chicken.” According to the author, this is adapted from a recipe featured on the menu at the café at Stone Barns Center for Food and Agriculture — a farm-to-table mecca in Tarrytown, NY. She uses chicken thighs and legs, marinated for 24 hours in soy sauce, orange and lemon juices, honey, garlic, and smoked paprika, then grilled (or baked — both methods are described). I grilled mine — perfection! I LOVE this flavor combo, and it is super easy.
German Potato Salad
I had a bunch of white rose and Yukon gold potatoes from a CSA delivery on hand, so this was a no-brainer. A fairly basic no-nonsense German potato salad recipe with bacon, white wine vinegar, whole-grain mustard, some dill, and thinly sliced scallions. I cut the recipe in half to make 4-5 servings, but I’ll be doubling it soon to bring to a large neighborhood potluck picnic soon. Tasty, easy to prepare. A keeper for sure.

So there it is. One of the larger sections in terms of recipes is “The Walk to the Farmer’s Market.” The recipes feature fresh seasonal vegetables and fruits (“Grilled Duck Breast with Cherry-Peach Relish,” “Grilled Smashed Potatoes with Chutney,” “Pizza with Ramps and Taleggio” are all especially nice). And the Thanksgiving section has some nice alternative takes on traditional dishes (I’ll be making the Cranberry Relish recipe this year: it starts with the traditional cranberries and oranges but also includes pineapple, red onion, and fresh ginger).

I really do like this book, both for the narrative and stories about Rosenstrach’s own family rituals as well as the solid recipes. Solid 4 stars.

Very few pictures, and the ones that are included aren't all that helpful. Highly disappointed. My review includes a break down of pics included, chapter titles, what additional information the recipes include, and summary of thoughts.

Let me elaborate on my thoughts..

I cook. I am not a great cook, but I have been cooking for long time- much more than a novice but not a consistent pro. I can throw things together and make them taste pretty good, or I can follow a recipe with same results. I am adventurous eater, so I can visualize a lot of recipes. I have a small selection of cookbooks, and of course we all have the internet.. so when I add a cookbook to my collection it has to be special, and it really should included many pics. So yes, I rate based on wanting pictures with my recipes. It is what makes me decide on going through the effort to make someones recipe- that I can see it and taste it in my mind through use of my eyes first. It also helps to know what something should look like. Helps me decide if the textures depicted in pictures are what I want at that time. I can not say enough how important, helpful, and saleable a recipe book WITH PICTURES is. If need be, if one didn't want a pic per page- then put a punch of thumbnail pics on a page with title of what it is. Or at least a representation of most recipes. But if there is a section with 20 recipes, and there is but one pic? One thing to be a book on how to cook, and how to perhaps cook the classics (whether meat or plant based)- but to have an assortment that has some unique traits to it.. how can anyone fully visualize it. So to not have and pics, or to have so few (and what is there is still not helpful). Show me what the heck Japchae is instead of the glob of coconut whipped cream that is included. Really? Pretty sure we can all visualize that.

Ok, so here are the PICTURES THAT ARE INCLUDED:
--Nice cover photo- I'd eat that! is that recipe included? Could have specified what it is. After hunting through book, I deduced that it is called, "Sweet Corn Butternut Squash Flatbread"
-Picture of spiralized carrots
-Picture of a mix of spiralized stuff
-Another pic of just raw spiralized stuff
-One more pic of raw spiralized stuff
-Sorry, yet another pics of raw, spiralized vegetables

CHAPTER pics- There are nine recipe chapter with several recipes in each chapter- easily 10-20 recipes per. Many lost opportunities to show us what these recipes look like.
-Pic of "Cinnamon & Sweet Potato Waffles" at beginning of BREAKFAST chapter
-Pic of "Banh Mi Bowl" at beginning of BOWLS chapter
-Pic of "Garlicky Sage Oven Fries" (spiralized potatoes) at beginning of SNACKS & SIDES chapter
-random Pic of spiralized sweet potato.
-Pic of "Sweet n' Spicy Beet Wraps" at beginning of SANDWICHES & WRAPS chapter
-Pic of "Sweet n' Spicy Fruit Salad" at beginning of SOUPS & SALADS chapter
-Pic of "Spicy Soft Tacos" at beginning of MAINS chapter
-Pic of "Pumpkin Spiced Apple Parfait" at beginning of DESSERTS chapter
-Pic of "Mango Harbanero Seltzer Water" at beginning of DRINKS chapter.
-Pic of "Coconut Whipped Cream" at beginning of STAPLES & SAUCES chapter

To further break down the book in case the lack of pics isn't an immediate deterrent:
Introduction
Chapter 1, pg 1: Ribbons, Spirals & Shoestrings
Chapter 2, pg 13: Breakfast
Chapter 3, pg 35: Bowls
Chapter 4, pg 55: Snacks and Sides
Chapter 5, pg 69: Sandwiches and Wraps
Chapter 6, pg 87: Soups and Salads
Chapter 7, pg 105; Mains
Chapter 8, pg 141: Desserts
Chapter 9, pg 163: Drinks
Chapter 10, pg171: Staples and Sauces
... finishes up with charts on Measurement Conversion, Resources, and Index

-Recipes include how servings, prep time, cook time

-Recipes include info stating such information such as Nut Free, Soy Free, Gluten Free, Under 20

-Recipes include info stating nutritional information such as calories, total fat, total carbs, fiber, sugar, protein, sodium

-Some recipes included Prep or Ingredient tips.

At first glance, I just sort of skimmed book and felt the desire to put it down. If I were in a book store, that is exactly what I would have done. It would have been a passing glance. No matter how good the recipes may be, I would pass over this book. Since I am reviewing it- I picked it back up and began reading the recipes. Trying to visualize them and taste them in my mind. There are a few that I am keen on trying. But many I will pass on.

The font for the letter "T" looks like an "F" at times, which gave me a giggle one one of the recipes looked like it reads "lemon-mango fartlets", or "fangy cucumber salad"

If a redo is considered, I would include pics, organize the pay layout- pare it down to one page per recipe, clearer font, and perhaps a few more novel sounding (looking!) recipes. Give me something I can't already find super easy on an internet search.
Great books that I intend to read again.  I’m only disappointed the text was downsized rather small which makes the language, already complex, somewhat more difficult to read.

Very disappointed not scary at all

I've had this in my "to read when I have time" stack for quite awhile, and being laid up with a really nasty cold for the past 9 days gave me the opportunity finally to pull it out of the pile.

It was a lot better than I thought it would be. It's a different take on the "vampire" genre -- I'm not usually into books with a "supernatural" theme, but this one sounded unusual enough that I decided to give it a shot. I'm glad I did.

The protagonist in this case -- the vampire -- is Nathaniel Cade, a 140-yr-old secret service agent sworn to protect the President forever. Yeah, I know -- it sounds crazy, but it works. There's dark humor, lots of action, a whole "good vs evil" thing, a lot to hold one's interest.

The plot isn't overly complicated, but the whole thing is well written, with characters that are nicely "fleshed out." I'm not sure the "Twilight"-lovers will love this, but if you're looking for something along the lines of a "political action thriller" with a twist, you could certainly do a lot worse than this.

I did not really care for this book and I wouldn't read it with my child. I felt that it had some poor lessons in it. It stars out about talking about how most teachers are strange... when you are working hard to get your kids to respect teachers, I don't think that's a great way to teach. So the teacher, who seems to be a witch, then teaches the kids with her magic, but I find many of the lessons to be poor. Like how she uses magic to cheat and get them to win a game. It shows other teachers in a bad light trying to catch her doing something bad and she retaliates against them. It is extremely short with large print and pictures so even if you like it, it is way shorter than the chapter books. I'll be looking for a different series.

A little too woo for me. Celery juice will fix everything?

What I did like:
I found some of the information pertaining to viruses setting up in areas of the body which create the auto immune issues very interesting, especially as it pertains to me. I plan to do some more research on that as it was not delved into as deeply as I would like- so it was starting point information, and not anything that I could immediately buy into (despite wanting to.. so I have to do extra homework beyond the book that is meant to help me learn- and not much in the way of citations).

What I didn't like:
The book tends to repeat a lot with non essential information. I don't mind context, but it felt like I was being sold to, rather than educated. I am not really interested in the other books. That was kinda pushed over and over in little ways.

The beginning is rather arduous- I'm like, get to the point already (sorry mega fans). This is one of the few times that I recommend reading or watching a truncated version. I LOVE to read, I love to read more than I have to just to have context, I was very open minded to learning about the benefits of celery juicing.. but this book is an overall miss for me. Felt like one long advertisement.

****To be clear- I am not doubting there are benefits to consuming celery juice. It might be amazing for you-- we are not reviewing celery juice, but the book itself. I am not even against anyone trying the plan outlined in the book. I am not at all familiar with the writer or what their accomplishments or accreditations are. So in that regard I am unbiased going in. I preordered the book on a whim out of curiosity. I'm just sharing that the overall experience of the book, and the information contained within (and the misinformation, I suspect) left me uneasy. Curious on some things, but a little uneasy on others. Is taking collagen powder really a terrible thing?

Do the juicing, but can do so without reading the book. Really.

This is essentially a sale's pitch for his other book, which you have to buy separably. Don't waste your time or money. There are better books out there. I'm pretty sure that the 5 star reviews must be shills because this book is horrible.

I bought this book because I'm in love with the new TV show Houdini & Doyle. I was totally intrigued by the first few chapters where the author pointed out the similarities and differences in how each was raised. By the time I got to the middle of the book, I'd had enough. The language is very stiff and it read too much like an academic lecture. These were two brilliant, creative showmen, but the book didn't have any of the spark that these men embodied in their lives.

I also thought the author got way off track spending a lot of time with the history of spiritualism and the timeline was very confusing. Sadly, not the read I was hoping for.

What a dilemma today's busy cooks face! Our choices seem to be fast food, slow cookers, or "can of this/can of that" recipes that, let's face it, aren't as wholesome as they could/should be.

Easy Weeknight Dinners provides one solution: easy to prepare recipes that use *mostly* whole ingredients, using kitchen tools/utensils that most of us have and easy to find components. "Whole," in this case, means things from the departments that usually line the perimeter of the supermarket: fresh produce, meats, dairy, etc., avoiding mostly heavily-processed canned foods. Not that it's totally avoided: chicken stock, for example, might come in a can or a carton. Olives, pickles, etc. -- no need to make those from scratch. But the theme here seems to be preparing dishes that are easy and reasonably healthy, too.

I found a number of excellent meals that would be a snap to prepare even after a long day. I'm lucky to live in the Pacific NW where fresh fish is plentiful year round. The Salmon with Vegetable Salsa on page 139 was dynamite! Super tasty, and from fridge to table in just under 30 minutes. I made the Cinnamon Apple Pork Chops on page 112 -- again, just under 30 minutes start to finish. Bacon & Rosemary Chicken (page 117) came together easily with great flavor and paired great with a spinach salad made a great meal in no time at all. There are some slow-cooker recipes, dishes prepared on the grill, desserts -- it's all here, and easiest enough for a novice cook to tackle.

Yes, there ARE some recipes in here that fall back on canned cream of mushroom soup/etc., and those serve a purpose once in awhile. But there are so many good recipes that don't use those "shortcuts" that make this a good choice for those who really want to cook more "from scratch," but are afraid they just don't have the time. This book proves that if you have 30 minutes and basic cooking skills, you CAN put a good healthy meal on the table.

No. There is some helpful info to be gleaned from this book, but it lacks substance. This is more of a recipe book, and one without pictures. There aren't pictures! well, maybe 3-4. Not enough to be helpful. I want to see what the recipe looks like finished, and when it comes to individual items being dehydrated--if this is meant to be a learning source-- then why does it not have pictures of what much of this is referring to. So I would say, not for newbies.

There is a page on best/safe practices. There is about 14 pages all told on the "101" of dehydrating.

Had I been in a bookstore, I would have picked this up for the title, and then would have set it right back down once I flipped through it. I dont feel better informed after having read through this. Blegh,
Have managed most of the easy stuff in cleaning out the past. The sage ritual & encouragement from this book may be enough for the last of it.

Well, if this isn’t a match made in heaven. A cookbook laid out in a way that totally matches how I think about getting food on the table day to day.

My usual line of thought is this: “What do I have in the fridge? Hmmmm … lessee …. uh yeah, that XXXXX looks good. Now, what can I do with it?” OR I’ll be at the supermarket, see they have a “deal” on something I like (chicken thighs? salmon fillet? apples?) so I’ll pick some up without having a concrete plan for what to do with it.

Enter “Dinner Made Simple.” Here you have 35 common ingredients listed in alphabetical order. For each ingredient, you get 10 different recipes. Not the same recipe with 10 variations; no, you get 10 DIFFERENT recipes. And they’re easy to prepare, using mostly pantry items you already have. AND they’re good!

My usual practice when reviewing a cookbook is to prepare 3 recipes to check for flavor, accuracy, and ease. Here are my three:

1) I had skin-on bone-in chicken thighs in the freezer from a supermarket special a couple of weeks ago, so I started with what I had on hand. “Chicken Thighs” is one of the 35 listed ingredients; 3 of the recipes were for bone-in chicken thighs; the other 7 were for skinless/boneless. I chose “Teriyaki Chicken with Bok Choy” because I just happened to have 2 bunches of baby bok choy from a CSA delivery the day before. Five ingredients, including bottled teriyaki sauce (I have a good one I like to use in stir fry, so I just used that). This one is super easy in terms of prep: marinate the chicken for 30 minutes in some of the teriyaki sauce + garlic, then roast in oven basting with additional sauce. Add bok choy in last 10 minutes. Serve over rice. Can’t get much easier than that. It was good, not “OMG THIS IS FANTASTIC!!” good, but good. Good enough to make again.

2) Next up: potatoes. That same CSA basket brought a bunch of Red Bliss potatoes. So I consulted the “Potatoes” chapter. The 10 recipes feature a variety of potatoes: new potatoes, Yukon Gold, Red, and Russet. I’ve been cooking long enough to know that most thin-skinned potatoes can be interchanged (i.e. Yukon Gold, Red Bliss, White Rose, “new,” etc.) because the “flesh” is a little less starchy than Russet. I decided to try the “Creamy Dill Potato Salad” as I had everything needed: potatoes, sour cream, mayonnaise, dill, lemon, scallions. A simple straightforward recipe, I halved it since I had only 1.5 lbs of red potatoes. This I served with a simple grilled salmon fillet seasoned with lemon and a little dill. Lovely! AndI had leftovers for the next day.

3) Finally: ground turkey — another sale item from the supermarket. I had about 1.25 lbs to play with. The “Ground Turkey” chapter has a variety of ways to use this versatile ingredient, and I chose “Pasta with Turkey and Broccoli.” I didn’t have the orecchiette pasta used in the recipe, but I did have bow ties. You cook the broccoli in the water with the pasta during the last minute (time saver there). Ground turkey seasoned with garlic, fennel and crushed red pepper, browned, then tossed with the pasta & broccoli plus a little olive oil and grated parmesan. Very simple, a little bland for me — I’ll increase the red pepper and garlic next time. But otherwise, solid, easy to prepare, and good the next day as leftovers.

Is this the perfect book for everyone? Hardly. The crux of the focus here is *simple* recipes (nothing too fancy or “haute cuisine”) that serve as a jumping off place for quick meal preparation. I’m using this more than I thought I would. Solid 5 stars from me.

As a cookbook collector, I was happy to read and review this collection of reader favorites from the Washington Post when it showed up on my Netgalley list.

I found it to be a very mixed bag: some recipes immediately caught my interest; others, not so much. For example, I didn't find much in the Starters chapter that I really wanted to try. In some cases, the ingredients combined with the amount of time and effort needed to produce the finished product just didn't seem worth it. I did make "Carol Mason's Herbed Gougères," and they turned out WONDERFUL. Will definitely be making these again.

But the collection really shines in the Meat chapter, with a half dozen recipes I immediately added to my "must try soon!" list. I've already made "Brown-Sugar-and-Beer-Braised Beef," "Mahogany Short Ribs," "Misery Meatloaf" (I'm a sucker for a good meatloaf!), and "Pistachio and Pomegranate Meatballs." Each of these recipes could be made easily by a good beginning or intermediate cook, but they're impressive enough for a group of "foodies."

A bit more involved is "Ann Marotto's Fresh Tomato Pie" from the Meatless chapter, but it's so worth it. I served this for a light summer lunch, with a side salad and a fresh crisp Pinot Gris. From the Poultry chapter, I've made "Lemon-and-Honey-Flavored Chicken," using bone-in chicken thighs. This is one of those wonderful "toss everything into a pan, and bake"-type recipes -- very easy, great flavors, etc.

Not every recipe in this book will appeal to everyone, but you're bound to find something that you'll like -- in fact, you'll probably find several "somethings" that will tempt you to head to the store and into the kitchen. Would make a nice gift, too. A solid 4 stars.

This is a nice introduction to filet crochet. The designs are fairly simple to execute; all are square, so no end-of-row increases/decreases to mess with. They're all about the same size, too: in size 10 crochet cotton, they end up around 16" square. Nothing terribly elegant, most of the designs are fairly "coloring book" in style -- a cactus, house, butterfly, lighthouse, a rose, etc. There are clear instructions that explain the meaning of the symbols in the charts, and the written instructions explain how to work a chart top to bottom (or bottom to top). I'd say these would be perfect for an advanced beginner to thread crochet, but would likely be a bit of a challenge to someone with not much experience.

If you're looking for elegant artistic filet designs, this probably won't work for you. But if you're interested in trying filet and want a fairly easy pattern to help you "get your feet wet," this would probably work nicely.

Many years ago, when I lived for a time in the deep South, I had a subscription to Southern Living magazine, mostly for the recipes. As I moved over the years, I moved on to magazines like Washingtonian and Sunset. But I kept a number of issues of SL and even purchased several of the recipe annuals.

This is a new addition to the Southern Living cookbook series, but with a nice twist: take a tried-and-true popular Southern Living recipe from years past, and give it a makeover to fit 21st-century tastes and lifestyles.

Recipe Revival contains over 200 recipes, half of which are originals from the 50-yr history of the magazine (it first started publishing in 1966). The updates pay serious homage to their past, but are definitely fresh takes on old favorites. For example, “Orange Rolls,” yeast rolls from the 1970s morphs into “Citrus Pull-Apart Bread.”

The recipes are divided into standard cookbooks categories: Breakfast & Brunch, Lunchtime, Cocktail Hour, Dinnertime, Sideboard, and Desserts. There are two indices: “Vintage Index,” which lists the original recipes by decade (1960s, 1970s, etc.); and the standard ingredient/recipe title index.

So how are the new versions? My usual practice is to select 3 recipes to make in order to check the ingredients, accuracy, and ease of following the instructions. For this book, I found 3 recipes I’ve made several times over the years from the original Souther Living magazine, and tested the updated versions. Here’s what happened:

1. Caramelized Onion Quiche (pg 38 - original: Perfect Quiche Lorraine)
The original SL Quiche Lorraine recipe has been my go-to recipe for Quiche Lorraine ever since I first saw it in the 1970s. It is a fairly standard quiche lorraine recipe — bacon, swiss cheese, cream, eggs, baked into a pastry crust — and makes a large 10” pie, with 6 generous servings.
The new version uses sweet onions (like Vidalia), bacon, gruyere cheese, eggs, and cream, with lots of fresh green herbs. I used sweet Walla Walla onions (that’s what came in my CSA box a few days ago), and caramelized the onions in the bacon drippings (instead of olive oil), but otherwise followed the recipe exactly as written.
This is definitely a refinement of the original without diverging too much. I love the addition of the onions, and it still makes a large 10” pie, and the basic steps are almost identical. This is a keeper.

2. Farmer’s Market Pasta Salad (pg 92 - original: Tarragon Pasta Chicken Salad)
The original is another favorite: shell pasta, diced chicken breasts that have been marinated and baked with a garlic and tarragon dressing, combined with diced bell peppers, green onions, celery and dressed with reserved marinade. This is easy to make with little prep and very tasty.
The updated version is a chicken pasta salad, but VERY different with smoked chicken peaches, zucchini, fresh tomatoes, fresh corn kernels, bell pepper, and green onions. Instead of tarragon, the parmesan vinaigrette dressing contains fresh basil and cilantro. Since the chicken is pre-cooked, there’s no marinating except that the veggies sit for 10 minutes in the dressing.
This is a good chicken pasta salad, but I wouldn’t consider it so much an update of the original as a different riff on “chicken pasta salad.” It’s a keeper, but won’t replace my favorite tarragon chicken salad.

3. Hugh’s Southern Mac and Cheese (pg 250 - original: Macaroni and Cheese)
The original Macaroni and Cheese recipe from the 1980s was an adventurous combination of ham, onion, cheese, and macaroni along with cottage cheese and sour cream. I kept this recipe around since it’s such a different take on the more “traditional” mac & cheese.
The updated version omits the cottage cheese and sour cream, substitutes bacon for the original ham, leeks instead of onion, and two kinds of cheese (cheddar & gruyere). It’s good — actually a little less complicated that the original, more “refined” flavor — very good, indeed. A keeper and it will probably replace the original in my recipe repertoire.

So there it is: 3 keepers, two of which will likely replace the originals. This is representative of the book: some remakes bear little resemblance to the original except in a few ingredients; others stay close to the original but replace some ingredients with more contemporary favorites.

Solid 5 stars — I’ll likely keep this on my kitchen bookshelf for awhile as I work through other older favorites and their new versions. There’s a lot of good stuff here.

The editorial reviews and title for "A SystemC Primer" seem reasonably accurate. It's definitely a primer level of text, and there are numerous examples throughout. Some I would consider a bit basic, but then I've done a LOT of hardware design, and for someone in school or starting out, I would think basic is good. I should note for completeness, that there is also a SystemC IEEE standard in process, and that would be a good adjunct to this book, as it gets into more depth and detail on the language. Overall though, the Primer gets the job done as a Primer. I do have some negatives, unfortunately:

(1) The price (currently $80+ at time of writing) is a little outrageous (granted, a trend in college level texts, not unexpected, but really still a bit expensive for what you're getting here),

(2) the Primer is a bit weak in the high-level aspects of SystemC and using C++ (which is where some of the power is, high or architectural level descriptions as versus lower level languages, using SystemC for gate level stuff is going to be occasionally necessary but mostly inappropriate), and

(3)"SystemC from the Ground Up" might be the better first purchase (a little less expensive and a bit more breadth, but less an entry level primer)

I finally bit the bullet and purchased a spiral slicer -- in my case, the [[ASIN:B0007Y9WHQ Paderno World Cuisine A4982799 Tri-Blade Plastic Spiral Vegetable Slicer]]. The great thing is that the recipes in this book work just fine for ANY spiral slicer.

And the recipes are VERY good!

I always make at least 3 recipes from each cookbook I purchase, and I did that with this one.

First up: the Pesto Sun-Dried Tomato Egg Muffins. You "spiralize" a large Yukon gold potato, then briefly pre-cook the potato noodles with garlic powder (I used finely minced fresh garlic). The "muffins" are a combination of egg, the pre-cooked potatoes, wilted fresh spinach, and chopped sun-dried tomato; bake, then top with pesto. Yum!! I'm a sucker for anything with a lot of flavor, and this has a lot of flavor. Plus, the "muffins" can be frozen, then reheated in the microwave. I'll make this again.

Next: Sweet Potato Carbonara. It's just like it sounds: a "standard" carbonara recipe, but with sweet potato noodles instead of spaghetti. Yes, it has a different taste, but I really like it. I reduced the ingredients by 1/2 to make two servings (instead of 4), and it turned out just fine. Love the combination of pancetta, onion, garlic, and cheese with the slightly sweet orange sweet potato noodles. Really good! Ate half, saved half for another lunch a couple of days later.

Finally: Spicy Garlic Crab with Parsnips. OK, I modified this, and used jumbo shrimp instead of crab because that's what I had on hand. All other ingredients and instructions were done according to the recipe. I LOVE parsnips, and consider them among the most underrated vegetables out there. I use it in stews, casseroles, etc. It has an almost sweet taste when cooked, and is just so yummy! This is one of those super simple recipes that will be useful when I need something quick and different. Will definitely make this one again.

I feel that I've just tapped the surface of the wealth of tastiness in this cookbook, and look forward to trying more (and playing with my spiral slicer). Solid well-earned 5 stars!

I've been coloring as an adult since the 1980s, starting with some of the early Dover coloring books and colored pencils. It seems to have become a "thing" now, and I'm thrilled with the new material on the market. But you really can't beat Dover in terms of design and price.

This recent release by Dover is a prime example of the "new" Dover style, appealing more to adults. Marty Noble has been designing books for Dover for a LONG time (I know I had some of his books in the early- to mid-1990s), and his work is always excellent.

The designs here are influenced by India, Africa, and the Middle East: lots of paisleys, peacocks, stylized butterflies and flowers. The designs are printed on one side only on decent quality paper, and each page is perforated to make removal easier. Bear in mind that most markers (especially alcohol markers, like Spectrum Noir, Bic Mark-Its, etc.) will bleed through, so if you're coloring in the book, be sure to put a sheet of scratch paper behind your design so you won't inadvertently ruin the next page. I'm working on a design at the moment with Bic Mark-Its, and I'm noticing just a hint of feathering (color spreading outside the line), not too bad, but enough that you'll want to use a light touch with your markers.

Most of these designs are highly detailed with lots of tiny "bits" to color, so if you're intimidated by that, Magnificent Mehndi Designs may not be the best choice for you. But if you're interested in trying some more detailed coloring, there are several designs that combine "wide open spaces" with high detail.

I'm really enjoying this book more than I thought I would, and it's now one of my top 5 favorites. Great designs, great quality, hours of relaxing creativity.

Very high-quality paper and design, a compendium of detailed drawings to color, all based on scenes from Thailand.

The pictures are printed on one side only of very good high-quality art paper, so you can feel free to use whatever coloring medium you prefer (alcohol pens, water-based pens, colored pencils, even watercolor pencils). There are 5 fold-out double size images, too. And the binding is constructed so that the book lies flat for coloring without damaging the spine. Nice touch, that.

These are very highly detailed drawings, so if you're not comfortable coloring in tiny tiny spaces, this might not work for you. But if you're looking for something a little different, this would be a good choice. The images are beautifully drawn and should provide hours of coloring enjoyment.

I was provided a pre-publication copy of Soup Swap for review purposes.

A very nice collection of soup, stew, and chowder recipes, with some creative sides and garnishes, not *too* complex, and a lot of variety.

The “soup swap” isn’t as popular in my neck of the woods as it might be other areas, so I wasn’t as familiar with the concept as others might be. Basically, a group of friends gets together, and each guest brings a pot of soup to share with the group. The host or someone is designated to provide a side dish or two along with bread and a simple dessert. Leftovers are divvied up and everyone gets a sample of each soup. Sounds like a great fun evening!

While I might not be familiar with the swap concept, I’m no stranger to soups. It’s my favorite meal during the chilly damp winter months here in the Pacific Northwest, when the days are short and I just want something warm and comforting. I’m single, so making soup usually means finding recipes that freeze well unless I’m doing a soup dinner for friends.

This book couldn’t have come at a better time. We’re moving into fall here, and my CSA (community supported agriculture) deliveries are filled with the last of the summer produce and the beginning of our favorite fall bounty. So I’m looking for recipes that will freeze well and give me a good start for fall/winter.

My general rating for cookbooks is based on what percentage of recipes I would actually want to prepare, and I try to make at least 3 recipes from every cookbook I review. I followed that practice with this one.

1. Corn and Sweet Potato Chowder with Saffron Cream (page 80)
My last CSA delivery included 6 ears of bi-color corn and 2 sweet potatoes — perfect! This one contains cream, so not a good candidate for freezing. Not a problem — it keeps nicely for a few days, and it’s tasty enough that I don’t mind having it for several meals in that time. This is not a difficult soup to make at all. You need two pans — one for the soup and one for the saffron cream. I didn’t use quite as much saffron as the recipe calls for, but it still had a lot of flavor. A winner, but one I’ll make only occasionally or for company since it can’t be frozen.

2. Pork and White Bean Chili (page 132)
Oh, yum! This is precisely my kind of recipe. Freezes well, tastes incredible, and despite the number of steps, really isn’t terribly complicated. You make a spice marinade and combine it with cubed pork shoulder, then tuck it away for a few hours to steep. (I did this overnight.) You then brown the pork and combine it with beans, tomatoes, beer (!), and the leftover marinade and let it cook for awhile until the flavors meld. Oh, yum! This is perfect and freezes well, too. Absolutely will make again this winter.

3. Skillet Cornbread with Chive and Brown Butter (page 154)
One of the side dishes, I’m a sucker for a good cornbread variation, and this is a good one. I used my 10” Lodge cast iron skillet for this, and it worked great. You heat the butter just until it starts to brown, then remove from heat and add chopped fresh chives. You mix up a fairly standard “sweet” cornbread recipe (buttermilk, cornmeal, flour, eggs, baking soda, a little sugar), bake for awhile, and then add some whole milk to create a sort of custard layer, so it is a little different than normal, but oh my — it really is wonderful! Goes great with the chili.

So there it is. Some of the recipes may seem a bit complicated and have a fairly lengthy list of ingredients. But as with most soups, a lot of the ingredients are seasonings — herbs, spices, aromatics like onion and garlic — and once you get everything gathered and measured out, putting together a good soup really isn’t too tricky. Overall, solid 4 stars: I like it.

I first encountered Mary Werst's designs in her earlier book, "Extra-Special Doilies," and fell in love. Her designs are intricate, reminiscent of Patricia Kristoffersen, but still different and "modern."

This book features similar patterns, although they're probably a little more "dense" than the ones in the earlier book, and solid rather than lacy. As such, they take a little more thread than other lacier doilies. But the end result is wonderful! These are NOT for novices. You really need some experience working more complex stitches (front- and back-post stitches, split clusters, various types of decreases, etc.). If you've ever done a Kristoffersen pattern, you'll feel right at home here.

in the interest of full disclosure, i did not buy this book. however, i read it in the course of about an hour while sitting at the bookstore. you too should read it for free, if at all.

first off, the author has no real love motorcycles, but the neo-harley dentist/accountant scene that the "american" pig iron scene has become. the photos of him and his friends are enough to make me want to avoid any of the rides, bars, rallies, and scenes that the book describes. frankly, dear author, with your stringy long hair, gauntlets, black leather, and overweight motorcycle, you all look a bit light on your toes. and don't bother with the sportsters, they aren't heavy enough, expensive enough, and can actually be ridden.

second, the cavalier attitude about drinking and riding is disgusting. the main point given to not do so is financial. never mind that riding a bike so large that one can barely control it under the best of circumstances is dangerous, add some alcohol to the mix and create a dangerous situation for everyone. please, dear author, maintain also your cavalier attitude about wearing your helmet and do us all a favor.

third, to state that harleys now have a modern engine is wishful thinking, at best. it is still the same 45 degree pig that it always was, prone to shaking itself apart and mismatched power stroke that makes one grind their teeth. even buell, dear author, has gone to a modern rotax engine that puts the harley engine to shame.

fourth, liberal use of the 'F' word does not a biker make. nor does a liberal sprinkling of 'custom' off the shelf, bolt on, chrome trinkets make a custom bike. i've been riding a long time, have completely disassembled my own bike and rebuilt it how i wanted it (and no, it isn't american and its not japanese), and nothing makes me more amused than to see a bunch of middle aged nobodies riding a bunch of off-the-showroom-floor pigs, all dressed in black and looking for all the world just what they are: overgrown boys that finally have the $30K to buy into a scene and maybe missed their exit for masquerade ball. oh, and trucking your bikes to the rallies and/or flying is about your speed. you earned those scratches on your bikes, buddy. gotta love it.

fifth, if motorcycle gangs don't like to be called gangs and/or gang members, they shouldn't behave as such. sorry, but any 'club' that sells drugs, prostitution, guns, and resolves disputes with violence as their main source of income is a gang. it is what it is, get over it.

lastly, the bullet lists were somewhat helpful. lists of things that one needs on the road, things to watch out for, etc., are about the only thing worthwhile in this waste of paper. but you have to look at skull and crossbones instead of proper bullets, oh brother.

do yourself a favor and read zen and the art of motorcycle maintenance. the reading is better, the author actually cares, and the lists are much more useful.

and the 'F' word simply isn't necessary to get your point across.
