I was trying to setup a studio for a multi-cam live Q&A web-broadcast, and this unit came highly recommended. Turns out this might not be the best choice, and the whole experience sort of leaves a bad taste in my mouth.
First the good:
The functionality of this device really impressed me! All I wanted was a live HDMI switch that would let me keep the audio separate (so it would be live regardless of camera active) and wouldn't blank the display when swapping inputs (which consumer hdmi switches do, as the connection re-negotiates). This unit goes far beyond that, using industry standard production controls for cuts and cross fades, title / graphic overlays, and even some basic Chroma Keying for doing live green screen (that was a surprise!). The little LCD is pretty neat too.
And now, THE BAD:
THERE IS NO HDMI OUTPUT. It only supports SDI, which no one mentioned to me when I said I wanted to toggle between HDMI sources. There is an HDMI preview which shows you multi-view (all the cameras in a grid), but no regular HDMI output. Only SDI, which is ridiculous to me. You have the HDMI port there, at least give us the option to choose whether it is multi-view or program out! Black Magic claims they didn't offer the HDMI out because the connection isn't reliable, so they want to push SDI as the output. But that isn't acceptable, the hardware is there, give us a choice! The result of this stream is going to an internet broadcaster which supports an HDMI input only! Now I'd need to purchase yet another accessory to convert the final product back into an HDMI signal so it can get where it needs to go.
Oh, and speaking of additional accessories that need to be purchased, THE BOX LACKS A POWER CORD. You have to source your own. That seems silly, its such an inexpensive thing that you expect to be there. Thankfully I have a cord from an old lighting accessory that I don't use anymore, but it seemed very odd that it was missing to me. Then, it did not come with a setup guide or instructions, only an SD card. That's fine, going green and all that, but I put it in my laptop and it came up blank. So now I have to find the software and manual myself. And at first, I found what I thought was the correct manual via Google only to realize it was for a different version of their ATEM products.
I know this is an inexpensive product for what it is, but let's be serious for a moment- it still costs nearly $1,000. For that amount of money, how much could it really cost to throw in a power cable and printed manual? $5? 10? Compared to the price of the unit, that's close to nothing. I also know that the buttons on front are because it is designed to be rack-mountable, but when you're setting this up on the fly and try to put on a tabletop, the controls are just awkward. It would have been a lot nicer ergonomically to have some controls on the top instead of the front side. But now that's just nitpicking. At the end of the day, I feel like this device is frustratingly close to what I was looking for, but the experience has been pretty crummy so far.

I have been working with security camera products for over 20 years - over that time I have installed systems for businesses, corporations and individuals - from enormous multimillion dollar systems to single camera nursery monitors. I’ve consulted on large projects and written about security vulnerabilities within networks for trade publications.

This is a field I know well. Over the years the technology has improved and become less and less expensive to produce. It is now practical to install security systems that would have only previously been used in commercial settings in residential.

I have tested and reviewed many wireless doorbell systems. Typically I always advise to avoid any wireless cameras due to the inherent security vulnerabilities but this is often not possible with doorbell cameras. That being said there are key components I look for in wireless doorbell cameras: clear picture that provides a wide viewing angle, it either be hard wired or have excellent battery life, when chime button is pressed alert is sent and app can be opened and responsive in a quick time, 2 way audio communication is clear on both ends, motion sensitivity is adjustable and accurate.

I have thoroughly tested this product over the last 4 months and in comparison to the others on the market around this price point this product has proven to be clearly the best.

In terms of what is excellent - the internal battery is long lasting and dependable - I have found that it will last nearly 2 months on a single charge. The vast majority of wireless doorbells use proprietary batteries which forces users to purchase secondary batteries typically costing $30-$50. This factor is a huge bonus.

The camera is quite good. Images are clear and the wide angle provides a full view of the area in front of the doorbell. The AI Smart Human detection is without question the best I have found. This is a huge feature that helps prevent constant false alerts.

The base station holds up to a 128GB SD card for video storage. This is awesome for multiple reasons. If the camera was stolen the evidence will be saved on the base station within your home and all of your recordings are stored locally. This is an outstanding system.

Unfortunately, as with many similar products, while the hardware is good the software needs some work. As is typical, I can not find any way to force this doorbell to work with better software. It is obvious this was designed on the Tuya platform though I have not found any way of integrating it. I would much rather use the Tuya app where I have the bulk of my automation set but this will not migrate.

The lag from the time the doorbell button is pressed until you can see who is there is mediocre. The FASTEST I’ve been able to get this to work is 18 seconds - and I’m using an iPhone 13 Pro Max with 512GB on a super fast 1G mesh WiFi with a node located within feet of the doorbell inside the door.

The 2 way audio is spotty. It sounds like ordering through a drive through where every 3rd word is audible. Since I don’t use these to have in depth conversations it isn’t a huge deal but should be noted.

This is definitely among the best wireless doorbells I have tested for a while. It is not perfect, the sound leaves room for improvement as does response times but overall this is a quality unit.

While the vast majority of the doorbells I tested I advised to avoid all together this one is one worth using.

I have set up, tested and reviewed more security systems and individual products than I can possibly remember. As with any component there are a host of factors to consider. In terms of cameras more megapixels is not always better. As someone who works with cameras for a living, I tend to occasionally misplace/lose my lens caps. Having a one-size-fits-all backup like this claims to be, is therefore intriguing to me.
What I found however, is that this fits alright for smaller sized primes (such as those with a front element of 52mm), but a lot of my zooms or larger primes with bigger front elements are difficult or impossible to fit over. Rear cap was also somewhat of a hit-or-miss; I own E-mount, EOS, Nikon F, Minolta A-mount and Micro4/3 lenses, and none of them are too large in their rear element, but some lenses get wide right after the mount and won't allow a cover like this to squeeze over easily. Some of the smaller primes, especially pancake lenses like the Canon 40mm STM, really can't fit both a front and rear "cap" like this since the entire lens would be engulfed in one end or the other.
On top of all that, even if it DOES fit over your lens, I'm cynical as to how how useful it really is. The purpose of a lens cap is to protect the glass from two things: getting dirty and getting damaged. For starters, the material is soft and won't prevent a hard object collision. I'm not exactly willing to test that theory but considering I can press in and feel the glass through the cover, I'm guessing a hard object in your camera bag could still hit it and crack/shatter/damage it. Perhaps the rubber would soften the blow, but a real lens cap would prevent any blow from happening in the first place!
For dust and debris protection, perhaps it is a little useful, however this sort of material tends to attract a lot of static cling. Just leaving it in my gear bag for a bit, I'm finding dust and hair clinging to it like a balloon. Now, it is possible that means the dust/debris will stick to the cap and not your lens, which is useful, however it doesn't give me a lot of confidence to see stuff stuck to the thing I'm about to use to protect my lens from more of that stuff.

I suppose it's good as a backup in my gearbag in case I lose another lens cap and want something to keep dust off it until I get a replacement, but otherwise I'd strongly recommend getting a proper front and end cap for your lenses.

I have set up, tested and reviewed more security systems and individual products than I can possibly remember. Without question my go to solution for home security solutions is a computer based, Blue Iris system with POE cameras run off of quality POE switches. This type of system is not only easy to operate but is highly secure.

As with any component there are a host of factors to consider. In terms of cameras more megapixels is not always better. For instance 2MP cameras often perform better in low light while 5MP will allow for more detail when lighting is good. One disadvantage of higher megapixel cameras is the recordings will take up more hard drive space. Depending on the number of cameras, this can become a larger concern.

Highlights of this camera include:

Lens: 3.6mm HD Lens provides a viewable angle of 80 degrees

POE: Power over Ethernet allows for one wire to be run to the camera that will handle everything - not only making installation easier but vastly more secure

Images are excellent and even low light images are better than most similar cameras in this price range

Set comes with what you will need (except Ethernet cable and system) to be hung in host situations

PoE compatibility - 802.3af - critical for using with software such as Blue Iris

Great build quality - this camera is well built and will hold up outside in all conditions

Issues I’ve discovered with this camera:

An issue to be aware of with all bullet style cameras is spiders love spinning webs in front of the lens. Just be aware of this so if this were to be hung in a difficult to reach location that will be an issue. With that in mind, any security camera should be hung no more than 8 or, at max, 10 feet high. Capturing a person that can not be identified committing a crime serves little purpose.

Setting the angle of this camera is a challenge - this is done with an Allen headed bolt that is exceedingly hard to access.

Audio is not available with this camera.

With all things considered this is a quality bullet style camera at an outstanding price. The sensor is decent quality, the build is solid and will hold up to years of outdoor use. The lens produced clear, crisp images both during the day and at night. POE wiring is easy and the most secure way to go.

While this camera won’t perform as well as more advanced cameras it is perfect as an extra camera for hard to reach locations. It connects to Blue Iris with no problem and has proven to be very dependable.

This is a product I would definitely recommend.

When I initially saw this camera it checked most of the boxes I look for when looking for security cameras. It is PoE, has H.265, ONVIF support allowing use with Blue Iris, Sony IMX274 Starvis Sensor. Once I received this camera and began digging into it I quickly realized certain important facts.

Most importantly, this is a rebranded Hikvision camera. This is among the companies that has been discovered spying on the US using their cameras - the firmware this camera comes with is long outdated and, in fact, can not be updated to anywhere close to what is current. This is far too risky for me to install on my system.

Any camera you install on your system should have the latest firmware installed and you need to have a good understanding of network security protocols. Cameras such as this one, that are older versions of cameras made by manufacturers that are blacklisted - while still being sold, people who understand the industry avoid them - are an invitation for hackers to invade your system.

While the specs on and price of this camera seem decent - this is not a camera I recommend. This poses far more risks than it is worth.

I have had the opportunity to review many headsets and headphones over the years and without question the recent technological advancements have aided this area by leaps and bounds.

I can not recommend buying Bluetooth 5.0 enough over previous technologies if your devices support it. Nearly all devices that are Bluetooth 5.0 capable, including this one, will be backwards compatible with earlier technologies as well.

While the listing boasts of incredible battery life my testing has proven that simply is not the case. Battery life is lacking with these headsets lasting on average under 3 hours each in my tests. It is easily charged using the included charging case which will allow the earbuds to be recharged a many times from its one full charge. Since the earbuds can be used together for stereo sound or individually you can potentially double play time by using one at a time. Even with this, 3 hours of playtime from wireless earbuds is way below average. Even having the case which allows charging on the go, this doesn’t mitigate the frustration of having to charge after a few hours of listening.

The charging case is cool. It allows for Qi charging of devices and will act as a portable power supply. The downside to this is the size and weight. Sometimes I want to travel light and this set does not allow for that.

After receiving, I made sure the case was fully charged then confirmed each earbud had a full charge. After verifying full power within each earbud I followed the simple pairing instructions and had no problems whatsoever. Once the earbuds are removed from the case they first pair with each other then you simply pair with your Bluetooth device.

These earbuds will require testing which of the 4 sizes of ear bud caps work best for the individual user. I typically use the medium size which with this set the medium small ones worked better for me. I purchase sets of replacement memory foam ear bud pads which I ended up replacing them with as they are more comfortable and simply work better. Even with having better pads these buds tend to fall out.

The sound from these headphones is mediocre - from the bass notes to the midtones to the treble - not terrible but also not great. I tested these headphones with a wide range of audio - from audiobooks to jazz, heavy metal, country, classical and blues - everything just sounded ok.

I have listened to both music and audiobooks through these as I moved throughout my home and interestingly while these claim to be Bluetooth 5.0 they do not have near the range of other Bluetooth 5.0 sets I’ve tested. I estimate the effective range at around 20 feet.

While these are ok for listening to music, how are they for calling? The sound through these headphones is again mediocre- on both ends.

Controls are overly complicated and the buds do not always respond to the commands given which is especially frustrating when listening to an audiobook as I often skip ahead or back not intending too.

After fully reviewing these headphones over the last couple of months I have to recommend passing on this set. They are simply overpriced for the quality.

Let's get one thing straight right away: THESE ARE NOT SUNGLASSES. They are blue-light blocking at best, or just mildly tinted most likely. I took them out of the box and thought "Oh cool! These are the kinds of glasses that get dark outside and transparent when you are inside!", but nope- I wore them for a full hour outside and they didn't get any darker or exhibit any sun blocking properties AT ALL. So that's false advertising right there. They aren't for use in the sun. They don't darken anything.
As far as audio quality... they are a cool concept, but again, falls short of expectations. The open ear design means the speakers have to work harder to be audible. That means they often make those tin-sounding rattles that tiny speakers make when cranked up too high. This is definitely NOT cool for music as most of the fidelity is lost right off the bat, and adding insult to injury is that they often don't get loud enough to use outside. I'm not saying using it in a crowded subway terminal (that you can just forget about completely), I'm talking about even just going for a stroll around my suburban block with some birds chirping and the wind blowing lightly, maybe a car driving in the distance. I tried listening to a recorded lecture (since music was a lost cause), and they couldn't get loud enough to hear clearly. The microphone for calls was OK in my test, so I guess they are cool as a way to take a phone call without wearing a headset, but that only works if you're wearing these anyway as sunglasses. They aren't sunglasses, so you wouldn't be wearing them anyway, and you wouldn't be wearing them for music, and you wouldn't be wearing them to make a phone call either because they rarely get loud enough unless you are home alone... in which case you can put your phone on speaker and avoid this whole mess.
In conclusion- its a good idea but executed poorly and does NOT live up to expectations. I'm surprised there are so many good reviews on this product. I can't think of a time I would use it.

I had high hopes for this docking station from StarTech but, thus far, I’ve been pretty disappointed.

Like many people I like the portability of working from a laptop but when I am at my workstation I want more screen space to work on - I thought this Docking Station would be a perfect solution. Well, in theory, it would.

Inside the box you’ll find the docking station, a 3.0 USB cable, a power adapter with snap on plugs to fit various world power sources, a DVI to VGA adapter, and a CD-ROM.

Unlike many docking stations or hubs this is not Plug and Play. I’m running the most updated Windows 10 64bit and the CD-ROM is completely useless. After inserting it in my computer I received a message reading that it was not compatible with my machine. No problem, I went to the manufacturer’s website, found the product, clicked on support, downloaded the zip file containing the setup and updated drivers for my OS and continued.

I went through the installation process and it appeared to install fine. After accepting the user agreement it checked my system for compatibility - finding no issues it continued and completed installation. I have been working with security camera products for over 20 years - over that time I have installed systems for businesses, corporations and individuals - from enormous multimillion dollar systems to single camera nursery monitors. I’ve consulted on large projects and written about security vulnerabilities within networks for trade publications.

This is a field I know well. Over the years the technology has improved and become less and less expensive to produce. It is now practical to install security systems that would have only previously been used in commercial settings in residential.

As with all security systems my advice is never use cloud based recording - this opens your entire network to not only the company who you are giving blanket access too but also to hackers. The same is true for WiFi. Obviously WiFi is more convenient but if your privacy is a concern this really needs to be taken into account. Hackers have become quite adept at hacking into WiFi cameras and being able to not only watch your feed but control your cameras and access your entire network. If you choose to utilize WiFi cameras take the time to understand your router and employ proper VPN protocols.

Due to my security concerns with WiFi cameras my current security system is completely run through a dedicated computer utilizing the Blue Iris software. While this may seem intimidating to some, it is well worth learning and utilizing for the huge advantages in privacy and security.

I’ve noticed many people have asked if this camera works with a Blue Iris and it does without any issue. Just update to the latest firmware. The remainder of setup within Blue Iris is straightforward. This HITOSINO / VALUCAM 8MP IR IP CAMERA supports PoE 802.3af and will work with 48V active adapters which are more expensive, but you can also rely on a PoE switch, which in my opinion is a much easier, straightforward option.

The fixed lens size of 2.8mm provides decent field of view and the digital zoom is acceptable. The 1/2.7" CMOS image sensor is decent in low light, certainly not as good as 1/1.8” but I’ve found the low light capabilities average in comparison to other similar cameras. While low light capabilities will not blow you away they are reasonable. The LEDs must be illuminating objects to provide what is considered “colored night vision” which is misleading.

The camera has a built in microphone that allows for sound recording, though no speaker so two way communication will not be possible with this unit.

In terms of storage, the HITOSINO / VALUCAM 8MP IR IP CAMERA utilizes H.265 which is currently the best option for video compression. I have had this set up utilizing Blue Iris software which I definitely recommend. By fine tuning BI settings I have been able to find a balance between file size and image quality that works for my situation.

When all things are taken into account this is a solidly built camera, reasonably suited to outdoor environments - if at all exposed to elements rake the time to seal as an added layer of protection, decent daytime / nighttime resolution, easily mounted, and setup in Blue Iris was reasonably easy and straightforward.

While I would prefer to have pan and tilt capabilities at this price point that is unrealistic.

Overall I have been moderately impressed with this budget camera and would recommend it for many security situations.

For many years I have researched, written about and reviewed all types of security equipment - from the most advanced to the most simple. This set falls into the later category.

I have tested and reviewed several similar sets. Each of these sensors work based on a simple magnetic principle which is why it is critical they be mounted on the same plains, directly next to to each other when the door or window is closed. When open the magnet is released and the switch triggers causing the alarm to sound. This technology has been used with great success for many years.

The alarms are very loud and work the way they should. If the batteries lasted as long as the listing claimed this would be a reasonable set though they do not. These sets will require battery changes every 2.5-3 months, each sensor requires 3 AG13 or LR44 button style batteries. If all 6 sensors are utilized you will need 18 batteries every 3 months or 72 batteries a year. Not only does this get expensive but it is a hassle staying on top of the batteries always dying.

For those interested in utilizing these sensors, I recommend the following batteries: Eunicell SR44W 303 357 Silver Oxide Button Cell 1.55V Battery Long Shelf Life 0% Mercury (https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07MMTG4ZF/ref=cm_sw_em_r_mt_dp_7RB98F39642K4BXJ2Q2C) - while these are the same size and voltage they are made with better components and tend to last a bit longer and are far more consistent upon arrival.

After using these for 8 months I can say while these work and consistently sound when the sensors are separated there are far better options that use much more sophisticated, long lasting batteries. This is the type of product where you want a quality lithium ion battery that you can count on for many months or a year at a time - and ideally recharge it at that point.

After thoroughly testing this set I suggest looking into more sophisticated, sets that will cost slightly mor upfront but cost far less in the long run.

Being both an audiophile and a lover of all new tech when I first read about this technology coming out a few years ago I started looking forward to testing it.

So what are the potential advantages of bone conduction earphones? I’m glad you asked. When properly adjusted they allow the user to enjoy listening to whatever they choose while not blocking external noises, thus allowing the user to react to ambient noises. This should make them safe to use while driving or doing other activities that require hearing ambient noise in order to conduct safely.

As with other bone conduction headphones, these 9 Digital headphones allow for little fine adjustment. In order for this type of headphones to work properly and provide the highest sound quality they should rest flat, or almost flat against the skin between the front of the ear and the rear part of the cheek bone. When adjusting the speaker it will be clear when the speaker is properly adjusted. This set of headphones is designed to fall lower on the back of the head which for me pulled the speakers out of the ideal placement. While I have worked on adjusting this, it really is not possible. Due to this, sound quality is decent yet not optimal and surrounding people are able to hear what the listener is playing.

Just so it is understood, these headphones are rated IP53, meaning that they’re protected against dust, in so much as their inner workings won’t be compromised if dust were to get inside. They’re also protected against spraying water, with no pressure when tilted up to a 60-degree angle. This is a lower rating as compared to other headphones in this price range.

In terms of operation these headphones struggle to maintain a solid connection for anywhere close to the full 33 feet they are rated - typically Bluetooth 5.0 holds a connection with no problem though I have found that isn’t the case with these headphones. The batteries lasted approximately the amount of time listed. Sound quality is middle of the road especially due to not allowing for fine adjusting of the speakers.

This is a great technology with a lot of potential. I have tested and reviewed bone conduction headphones since they first were released a few years ago, due to how these fit and the connectivity issues this is not a set I would recommend.

The pencils also fit very snuggly in the boxes and it is difficult to get them out sometimes.
As for the "creaminess" of said pencils, I would say there are some inconsistencies in that as well. Some of the Floral colors are really soft and creamy, some of the browns and grays are hard and scratchy and then there are some that are just so waxy you can't lay down two colors on top of each other.
If you are a rubber stamper or crafter that plans to use them, you will probably be fine with these. They seem to be geared more to that kind of audience. The price is right for what you get. I had no trouble sharpening the few I used. No breakage like I have encountered with the Prismacolor set I've had for 10 years. The blending solution I suspect is just Gamsol which most stampers already have if they've been using pencils before. I was able to achieve the same results using the Prismacolor pencils I had over the markers.
I will say their customer service rocks because they are going to allow me to send these back. This is a great company to deal with, I just think they kind of missed the mark with these.

I have had the opportunity to review many headsets and headphones over the years and without question the recent technological advancements have aided this area by leaps and bounds.

I can not recommend buying Bluetooth 5.2 enough over previous technologies if your devices support it. Nearly all devices that are Bluetooth 5.2 capable, including this one, will be backwards compatible with earlier technologies as well.

Battery life is outstanding with these headsets lasting a solid 5 hours each in all of my tests. It is easily charged using the included charging case which will allow the earbuds to be recharged several times from its one full charge. Since the earbuds can be used together for stereo sound or individually you can potentially double play time by using one at a time.

This unique charging case is powered via an included super fast standard USB to USB Type C cable. This case will allow the headsets to be recharged even when you don’t have access to electricity.

After receiving, I made sure the case was fully charged then confirmed each earbud had a full charge. After verifying full power within each earbud I followed the simple pairing instructions and had no problems whatsoever. Once the earbuds are removed from the case they first pair with each other then you simply pair with your Bluetooth device.

The design of these headset makes it so it fits securely in your ear and is relatively comfortable. As someone who has used many of these units, this design is not the most comfortable as the weight is all on the ear canal. I’ve found headsets that are worn only supported by the ear canal can end up being painful when used for extended periods.

The sound from these headphones is decent - from the rich bass notes to the vibrant midtones to the bright treble. I tested these headphones with a wide range of audio - from audiobooks to jazz, heavy metal, country, classical and blues - everything sounded reasonable.

I have listened to both music and audiobooks through these as I moved throughout my home and it will easily pick up the signal through walls up to 25’ away, and even more without obstacles. This is a decent set Bluetooth headphones.

While these are great for listening to music, how are they for calling? The sound through these headphones is absolutely outstanding - on both ends. That is not always the case. Many of the other headphones I have tested, while they sound good to the person using it - those who they are speaking to can’t hear them clearly. It is no fun having a conversation where the other person keeps saying, “huh, what did you say?” You won’t have to worry about that with this set.

If you are looking for a decent headphone set, especially one at a reasonable price, with decent sound and reception and has the power to last this is a great choice.

The biggest difference between this projector & the much more expensive projectors in professional settings is the bulb. This one does a fine job in low light or with back light but will completely wash out with light around or over the screen. I ordered this primarily to do presentations & show videos at my club meetings. It performed well but we did have to fiddle a bit with the lights in the room & the position of the projector.

The usefulness of this projector is its portability & low cost. It is lightweight & compact & fairly easy to set up. I would have appreciated a padded carry case for the projector but the box it comes in works pretty well. It has foam that surrounds the projector, it holds all the cords, it has a handle & it is easily sturdy enough to set the projector on for more height.

It helps a lot with the focus & clarity if the projector is nearly horizontal. That means that if the projection screen is to be 4' over the ground then the projector also needs to be 4' off the ground which is generally going to be much higher than a table. The focus ring around the lens works very well to dial in the clarity. There is a foot that unscrews from the bottom front to angle the projector up. I tried propping something under the front to angle it higher but the image distorts & loses clarity the more it is tilted.

The biggest issue I have had with the projector is the image source. I can't get either of my laptop computers to recognize the projector as an external screen with the HDMI cable but the HDMI works on a DVD player. The IOS control works pretty well with my iPad. My Pixel phone doesn't support the built-in Miracast. The Bluetooth only recognizes my Samsung TV & none of my phones, computers or tablets sees the projector's Bluetooth signal. It has a YouTube app built into the device software but it is un-useable due to a cumbersome search process that doesn't return any results. I ended up using it with a USB thumb drive which works well & the menu is easy enough to navigate but takes some learning. I did not try the AV cable inputs or the SD memory slot.
Works great, compact, easy to use!

Personally I have been working with security camera products for over 15 years - over that time I have installed systems for businesses, corporations and individuals - from large systems to single camera nursery monitors. I work with a group of security professionals and we regularly consult on large corporate projects and write about security vulnerabilities within networks for trade publications.

This is a field I know well - I understand what to look for in quality cameras and the places manufacturers tend to try to save money on which makes an inferior product.

Over the years the security camera technology has vastly improved becoming less and less expensive to produce. It is now practical to install residential security systems that would have only previously been used in commercial settings.

This is advice any professional security analyst will provide and what we tell everyone of our clients. If you are new to home security camera systems, we recommend going with wired (non-WiFi), NVR or computer based recorders run with systems such as Blue Iris. This type of system is infinitely more secure and stable. While initial setup costs are a bit more the security it affords is worth every penny. It always pays to research the products, discuss them with those in the field, understand vulnerabilities and PLAN YOUR SYSTEM PRIOR TO PURCHASING ANYTHING. If you are at all tech savvy at all you can do it. While there is a bit of a learning curve it is not awful and in the end you will be thankful for the time you spent in the beginning as it will save you time, money and endless frustration.

I have set up, tested and reviewed more security systems and individual products than I can possibly remember. Without question my go to solution for home security solutions is a computer based, Blue Iris system with PoE cameras run off of quality PoE switches. This type of system is not only easy to operate but is highly secure.

While this camera is not natively a PoE camera I’m happy to share the 5 Pairs Passive PoE Injector and PoE Splitter Kit with 5.5x2.1 mm DC Connector RJ45 Power Over Ethernet by Exgoofit ( https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B07F82YK6P/ref=ppx_yo_dt_b_asin_title_o03_s00?ie=UTF8&psc=1 ) allow for this camera to operate almost identical to a PoE camera. The power adapter must be plugged in where the Ethernet cable is plugged in with the first pigtail adapter while the second adapter plugs onto the cameras power port and Ethernet jack. By utilizing this you can avoid the security concerns associated with WiFi cameras and still only have minimal wires run to the camera.

As with any component there are a host of factors to consider. In terms of cameras more megapixels is not always better. For instance 2MP cameras often perform better in low light while 5MP will allow for more detail when lighting is good. This camera is 2MP which is ideal for the vast majority of installation situations.

Highlights of this camera include:

Awesome Pan Tilt Zoom - Utilizing a 110° wide-angle varifocal lens and the head rotating 355° horizontally and 90° vertically, a 360° view is easily obtained. This makes this camera an outstanding choice to be placed on a corner of an eave where it can constantly monitor the largest possible area.

3.6mm Board lens - in my tests over the last couple of months I have been fairly impressed with the performance of this Fixed Focus 6mm lens coupled with the 1/2.8" Sony IMX307 CMOS with ICR image sensor. While this will not allow for near as clear zooming as a varifocal lens it is adequate in most circumstances.

Low Light Capability - this lens is decent in low light. Images are ok at low light, far better than the vast majority of similar cameras in this price range. IR lighting is dependable and nighttime / low light images are better than expected. My tests showed accurate images within 75’ of the IR lighting. By using both 4 IR lights and 4 bright LED lights this allows the camera to capture more details than many of the others on the market.

ONVIF Compatibility - critical for using with software such as Blue Iris. I have set it up with advanced features such as Patrol and Watchdog utilizing multiple detection zones.

2 Way Audio - I have had no issues utilizing the 2 way audio through Blue Iris - sound is accurate, clear and minimal lag.

Video Compression - This camera utilizes Smart 265/Smart 264/M-JPEG compression allowing for the smallest file sizes while maintaining the highest quality.

Great build quality - this camera is well built and will hold up outside in all conditions. That being said this is not a small camera in any way.

Over the course of my testing this product for the last several months I’ve been thoroughly impressed. It loads into Blue Iris without any issue and advanced features work without any trouble. By using the PoE splitter kit (referenced above) you can easily make this a secure camera by avoiding the use of WiFi. Build quality is excellent - this is made to last and hold up even in severe conditions.

This is a product I would definitely recommend. While this is the first ENSTER camera I have reviewed I look forward to seeing other cameras they create. All indications point to this being a manufacturer that builds high quality security cameras that are dependable and made to last.

Security Warning: All devices from Xiongmai, a Chinese OEM who manufactures white-label video surveillance equipment, come with an always-on cloud feature called XMEye P2P cloud. This feature contains serious vulnerabilities that allow attacks on millions of devices, even ones that are behind firewalls.

Millions of security cameras, DVRs, and NVRs contain vulnerabilities that can allow a remote attacker to take over devices with little effort, security researchers have revealed recently.

All vulnerable devices have been manufactured by Hangzhou Xiongmai Technology Co., Ltd. (often referred to as Xiongmai), a Chinese company based in the city of Hangzhou.

End users won't be able to tell that they're using a hackable device because the company doesn't sell any products with its name on them, but ships all equipment as white label products on which other companies put their logo on top. Evtevision is one of said companies.

If the product description mentions anything about "XMEye," then despite the logo on the front of the device, the equipment was made by Xiongmai.

As you can see in the third bulleted point at the top of the listing:

“Easy view on Smartphone via Xmeye, PC tablet via CMS,and Web browser IE.”

This is, without question, a Xiongmai device. Installing it or any of its software would place the privacy and security of you and your family in serious jeopardy.

Most security companies have banned selling these types of cameras for the reasons I have explained.

As soon as I opened this camera and started examining it I recognized some telltale signs and therefore did not set this up on my network. I caution everyone to avoid these products completely.

The built in camera and apps on the iPhone allow for so much more creative work than most of us lovers of photography ever dreamed possible - there is no reason not to enjoy that technology. Years ago I never would have dreamed of shooting photos with my phone rather than my DSLR but that is, in fact, often the case.

This has led me to testing, evaluating and reviewing many accessories that make creative shooting with the phone even more versatile than it is natively.

I’ve reviewed a few similar lens kits. This one comes with macro, wide angle and zoom lenses, a mini tripod for the zoom lens, the adapters needed to attach the lenses to the phone and a case for it all.

The wide angle and macro lenses are both decent quality - not professional quality but adequate for hobby enthusiasts. Of the three lenses the macro is the best. The wide angle causes photos focus to get soft towards the edges.

Unfortunately the zoom lens is sub par. While I can get it to work images are consistently below average. Zoom lenses work by utilizing a series of lenses that must be perfectly aligned to capture an accurate, crisp image. I had been anxiously waiting to try out the NETGEAR Nighthawk. I’d read over the impressive specs long before it was released, I’ve read glowing reviews touting it as the fastest, most powerful router Netgear had yet to produce... Specially made for the high demands of Smart Homes. All the hype made it sound like it was going to be AMAZING.

Now that I’ve set it up and run a full battery of tests - honestly the best thing I can say about it is it looks cool. 😕

I have been working with NETGEAR products for 25+ years so setup was not a big deal. As with most of NETGEAR’s new products don’t expect much printed material - that’s OK with me... I downloaded the Nighthawk app and followed the directions - they are fairly straightforward.

Restart your modem, plug in your Nighthawk Router, plug Ethernet Cable from modem to router, connect cell to router’s network using the ID and password provided on the sticker on top of the routers plastic cover, return to app. Here is where things began making less sense.

When I return to the Nighthawk app it goes through the process of connecting to my router and I receive an error message: ROUTER NOT SUPPORTED. Odd, but I’ve learned when installing a new router anything can happen. I exit the app and return, same message. I exit the app, restart the router, wait and restart the app and it recognizes. Great - I’m in... no big deal.

I update the Firmware, change the network name and password and start checking through the settings. I notice right away there are very few settings within the app - no security settings tab, no parental controls tab - odd, but I’ve learned NETGEAR tends to move these types of things around when they redesign their apps, so I put that concern to the side.

I plug my Ethernet cables into the 1G ports (there are 4 of them) in the back and watch the lights on front illuminate.

One of these runs to my computer and once it initialized the internet connection I ran a speed test - I’d tested with my previous router just prior to switch and was consistently benching at 210Mb/s download and 15Mb/s upload. With the Nighthawk I speed tested at 130Mb/s and 9Mb/s - definitely not what I’d hoped to see but I knew all my smart devices were working on rejoining the network so that could be slowing it down.

I decided to give it some time to let everything register on the network. In the Nighthawk app I had 42 devices and about half were still listed as unknown.

I let it work for about an hour and it recognized the majority of them... I ran another speed test - again through my laptop connected via Ethernet through the Nighthawk and this time received 154Mb/s and 11Mb/s. Still not great.

Then I went into my Nighthawk app as it has a speed test feature in there - I try that and receive an error that I must be on the network to run a speed test. I was on the network. I exit the app and go back in and receive the message ROUTER NOT SUPPORTED again. Ugh - really???

I reboot the router again and then the app recognizes it. While I’m able to control the router the speed test function does not want to work. Not a huge issue. I went through the browser on my phone and ran a speed test. The results were absolutely pathetic. This is the NIGHTHAWK - the router that is supposed to provide blazing fast speeds to WiFi devices and I received results of 5.4Mb/s download and 2.1Mb/s upload - and similar on 3 consecutive tests. At this point I’m starting to be less than impressed.

I decided to log in to the more advanced router settings via my computer. I had no problem getting in there but to my surprise there are ABSOLUTELY NO PARENTAL CONTROLS. This is the same company that has sold itself as allowing you to have complete control over every aspect of your home internet. NETGEAR Genie is no longer available and the NETGEAR Nighthawk panel doesn’t even have a section for parental controls - let alone work with Disney Circle.

While there are some advanced security features available within the NETGEAR Nighthawk Router controls on the computer they are anything but user friendly - they are hard to find, hard to set, and less than optimal.

As I was discovering all of this and researching these issues in the NETGEAR Community Forums I started receiving notifications from my smart devices that they were losing WiFi signal and then after a minute or two they would reconnect. This happened with my security cameras, LIFX lights, Wemo switches, and August door locks. After this went on for about an hour I decided my experiment with the NETGEAR NIGHTHAWK had reached its conclusion.

Unfortunately I can’t recommend this router at this time. While the hardware inside should have this out performing most any other router on the market I found it to be slow and glitchy.

I have had the opportunity to review many headsets and headphones over the years and without question the recent technological advancements have aided this area by leaps and bounds.

I can not recommend buying Bluetooth 5.0 enough over previous technologies if your devices support it. Nearly all devices that are Bluetooth 5.0 capable, including this one, will be backwards compatible with earlier technologies as well.

Battery life is outstanding with these headsets, lasting a solid 6+ hours each in all of my tests. It is easily charged using the included charging case which will allow the earbuds to be recharged 4 times from its one full charge. While many similar sets come with a charging case this one is tiny - having a built in 500 mAh battery, it will charge the headphones 4 times. The case has a dot display that shows the percentage of battery remaining so there is no surprise when it runs out of power.

Since the earbuds can be used together for stereo sound or individually you can potentially double play time by using one at a time.

This unique charging case is powered via an included super fast USB Type C cable.

After receiving, I made sure the case was fully charged then confirmed each earbud had a full charge. After verifying full power within each earbud I followed the simple pairing instructions and had no problems whatsoever. Once the earbuds are removed from the case they first pair with each other then you simply pair with your Bluetooth device.

These earbuds will require testing which of the 3 sizes of ear bud caps work best for the individual user. I typically use the medium size stereo caps but found the buds fell out of my ears with those. I switched to the small sized caps and, while better, I still had one fall out. I ended up using small cylindrical caps I had Can youand they fit perfectly.

Having no buttons on this set means controlling them requires touching either the right or left ear bud - this can be a bit finicky in terms of control.

The sound from these headphones is decent - while not amazing they are far better than others I have tested. I tested these headphones with a wide range of audio - from audiobooks to jazz, heavy metal, country, classical and blues - everything sounded decent but will not blow you away.

I have listened to both music and audiobooks through these as I moved throughout my home and it will easily pick up the signal through walls up to 35’ away, and even more without obstacles. This is really an outstanding set of Bluetooth headphones in terms of reception.

While these are great for listening to music, how are they for calling? The sound through these headphones is absolutely outstanding - on both ends. That is not always the case. Many of the other headphones I have tested, while they sound good to the person using it - those who they are speaking to can’t hear them clearly. It is no fun having a conversation where the other person keeps saying, “huh, what did you say?” You won’t have to worry about that with this set.

One critical area that I’ve had issues with concerning these headphones is they will randomly pair with my phone while charging in the case - I only realize it after I have missed calls and texts due to the sound being diverted to the headphones. I have no idea why these headphones do this but it has definitely proven a drawback to this set.

If you are looking for a small headphone set, that is comfortable to wear, provides decent sound and reception and has the power to last all day this is an ok choice. My primary concern is the persistent connecting when not intended causing calls and notifications to be missed. This issue has made this set one that I would pass on.

I bought this laptop because I wanted something that wasn't too heavy and could perform basic word processing and web functions. I had read product reviews for it before purchasing it and it seemed like it would suit me for what I required of it. After receiving the laptop computer I noticed two things immediately. The keyboard is made cheaply and to me, resembles something you might find on a first learning computer for a child. The keys have a cheap plastic-feel. After using the computer for two weeks, I already had to send it back to the manufacturer to have it repaired because two of the keys on the keyboard would get stuck and not produce the desired reaction when you push them.

Once I got the laptop back again, the keys were fixed but a few days later, the screen began to go black randomly and without warning. When it would come back, it would be in rainbow colors. After the second fix, it has been okay in those departments, but tends to overheat if you use it for any type of media, such as movies or music. The computer freezes a lot and has to be shut down manually at least once or twice a week.

I still think it is a bit heavier than I would like and since I have already had so many issues with it, I am actually afraid to carry it anywhere for fear that jostling it around in a carrying case might screw it all up again. The speakers on this computer are not nearly loud enough in my opinion.

What I do like about it is that it has pretty decent battery life (about 4-5 hours) depending on what you are doing at the time. I also like the colors the screen is capable of displaying when it is working correctly. I like that there are multiple USB ports for various endeavors. Overall I would say that this is a decent computer as long as you are prepared for the potential issues.

Everybody likes a little convenience in the kitchen. A main dish that requires preparation is certainly desirable, but a side dish can make the meal more complete and it's always nice when the side dish takes little time or work to prepare. Lipton Pasta Sides Chipotle Rosa works well when you want a simple addition to a main meal and don't want to devote time to preparation. Just add water, milk, and butter, boil for a short time, and serve- it really is that simple.

The convenience of this product is easily its best quality, but what about the taste? Well, it is spicy, Mexican- like, basic pasta flavored, and seems like a decent choice to add to a meal consisting of Mexican or similar foods. The spice level isn't too extreme, but I find the taste a little boring and a little too salty. A few more added ingredients to the mixture would make it better, but the manufacturer instead decided to keep it simple and included nothing but spiral- shaped pasta and spicy seasoning.

Based on flavor alone, this product would rank slightly below average, but it does offer some other qualities that help convince me to increase my rating. Besides the quick preparation already discussed, this product offers a good deal of nutrition- a level more on par with what you would expect in a healthy vegetable or other side dish. And, the price is right. It is common to find envelopes selling for less than a buck fifty each, so it easily fits within any budget.

A side dish can add greatly to a main dish and if quickness of preparation is your desire, then Lipton Pasta Sides Chipotle Rosa is a possible food option to consider. It isn't a great- tasting food product, but for the nutrition, price, and ease of preparing, it makes a good side dish option for your next Mexican dish or other meal.

Kid has been in need of powerful food thickening since 1998. ThickIt Concentrate (fka ThickIt 2... still blue lid container). With all the haywire shortages going on - toilet paper, anyone 🙄 - I figured it wouldn't hurt to have a 'back up plan' in case our preferred food thickener became unattainable. I also thought it might be smart to compare the two, as I couldn't find where anyone else had...

Whereas our preferred ThickIt 2 is modified corn starch (whether they modify by processing technique [something about an electrical 'zap'] or enzymes or chemicals, I don't know) Xanthan Gum is some "sugar building blocks fermented blah blah blah". For us, this doesn't matter.

For my purposes, I made 16oz of what I call "Gatorade Jelly" - it makes his beverage the consistency of thick pudding or slightly 'wet' mashed potatoes or sieved jam. Because the TI doesn't keep the liquid bind as a solid in the stomach, it's a good way for us to add fluids. (He NEEDS a GI tube but they won't because he's developmentally delayed and apparently the DD love to yank their GI tubes out daily. I usually put the 2 cups of Gatorade in a quart container with a screw on lid, add the 1/2c TI and shake violently for ≈20 seconds. Thickness is achieved within a minute to 90 seconds max. With the XG, mixing with cold beverage left white chunks. Had to remove to a blender and blend on high 30 seconds to get rid of the white chunks. Thickening was possibly more immediate than with the TI, but thickening didn't seem to stop. I used maybe 2 tbsp of the XG. Because of the costs and volume, the measures can't be compared by my math-challenged brain. The Gatorade with XG got so thick that I had to use a fork to get it to wash down my sink drain using 135°F water.
For us, that's gonna make the XG a Plan B only in the direst of circumstances.

As far as texture, the XG thickened Gatorade was, in all forms of thickness, slimy. Oral tactile sensitivity isn't an issue for us, but it's a nightmare for many.

Hope this helps another caregiver 💛

I've come to the conclusion, after trying dozens of 'caramel flavored' products (flavorings, flavored sugar free syrups, flavored syrups, drinks from the big name franchises, etc.) that NOTHING NOTHING NUHHHHH-THINNGGGG tastes like caramel except actual caramel.

Some people talk about bitter undertones and bitter aftertastes... for me, I've rarely noted bitterness in a caramel flavor product (unless it was with a sugar free product, in which case those artificial sweeteners - alone or in combination with the caramel flavoring chemicals - do have a bit of a bitterness sometimes); what I find is that the have a black pepper aftertaste. I don't know why my taste buds process the aftertaste that way, and I know it's weird - lol - but this product wasn't any different. It says it is super strength so I used a clean dropper I had and added a couple of drops... no caramel taste... a few more drops... maybe.... then literally two more drops and BOOM!! caramel-pepper.

I really wanted this product to be "IT" but it just wasn't for me. 3 stars instead of 1 because I know my tongue processes that weird taste whereas most folks are normal ;)

I love Indian food. I have several places in my area that I frequently turn to for take- out orders when Indian food is my family’s desire. But preparing our own Indian food is also enjoyable, especially when I can find a way to make the food without much effort. A good way to achieve Indian food without much work is Alcoeats Indian Simmer Sauce Spice Mix.

I have tried three of these powdered spices: Butter Chicken, Sauteed Veggies, and Royal Paneer. Each one is good for adding the necessary spice to make a meal taste like authentic Indian food. The mixes are good from a nutritional standpoint, with my only concern being the sodium content.

Of the three I have tried, I like Royal Paneer the best because it seems to pack the most flavor. The other two, not so much. In fact, I found both of the other two a little too mild. We immediately started to turn to other spices and add- ons, to improve the overall flavor.

Still, Alcoeats Indian Simmer Sauce Spice Mix is a good choice when you want a quick Indian meal. It doesn’t take long at all to prepare the sauce and it does make an ordinary meat or veggie dish taste better. The flavor isn’t as intense as I would like, but Alcoeats is still a good starting point when you want homemade Indian food in minimal time.

Parents want their kids to maintain a healthy diet but with sugary foods ranking so highly among the foods that kids love best, this can be a challenge. There are, fortunately, snacks that meet the challenge and one of them is Oatmega Kids Protein Bars Chocolate Peanut Butter.

These bars are small and are made with plants. They include a good deal of protein for such a small amount of food, plus Omega- 3 and other desirable qualities. There are only a few grams of sugar per bar and not many calories, so the bars easily pass most parents’ requirements. The calories, however, for a snack of such small size are actually more than I expected so you need to keep servings under control.

With a flavor like chocolate peanut butter, it would seem like a parent can’t go wrong with these bars, but after tasting them myself, I can say that they lean toward the bland side overall. They aren’t very sweet, which is great from a nutrition standpoint because the sugar is low. But from a taste perspective, they seem a little too unsweet for this type of product. Also, the chocolate and peanut butter flavors are quite subdued. They are not bursting with taste, like I had hoped.

I want my kids to eat better and Oatmega Kids Protein Bars Chocolate Peanut Butter are a good choice for parents who demand nutritional quality for their children. Many kids won’t be thrilled with the flavor since it is rather tame and the small size won’t satisfy the appetite of bigger kids. So, taking all factors into account, Oatmega Kids Protein Chocolate Peanut Butter bars are an average product overall.

Owning a Keurig makes it easy to serve up a piping hot cup of coffee or tea. Just turn on the appliance, pop in a K-cup, press the button and walla- a cup of steaming hot beverage is ready to drink. There is no waste and you make only what you need so nothing goes to waste.

Lipton K-Cup Iced Tea Lemonade is as easy to use as any other K-cup. It carries the Lipton name, so it has a solid reputation behind it. These are all good things, but I have to say that the beverage itself is only so- so in taste. It’s a little on the sweet side, at least for me. I usually drink my tea unsweetened, but I can handle a small amount of sweetness. This one goes a tad far in the sugary direction so it doesn’t necessarily suit my preference.

Aside from the sweetness, Lipton K-Cup Iced Tea Lemonade is also somewhat artificial in taste. I tried a few different cups, hoping that perhaps I got a bad K-Cup. But each cup of tea tasted about the same as the last. The flavor seemed a little off- not bad, but not anything amazing and not as good as some of my favorite all- natural, tea- bag style teas or other K-cup teas.

I drink quite a bit of tea and I love the convenience I get with my Keurig. But not all K-cups are worth drinking and Lipton K-Cup Iced Tea Lemonade is one of them. It isn’t terrible, but the artificial flavor and sweetness level convince me to rate tis product below average.

A refreshing beverage can go a long way in making a meal better or making a hot day more bearable. Carbonated beverages are usually a little better in terms of refreshment I tend to like drinks more when they are low in calories and sugar. So, I decided a good beverage option for me would be Alta Palla Grapefruit Sparkling Water.

Examining this beverage from a nutritional perspective reveals a long line of zeroes: Zero carbs, zero calories, zero protein, zero sugar, etc. This is all well and good, just keep in mind that, like most any other zero calorie and carb beverage, there is also zero nutrition. However, on the positive side, there is also a very short ingredients list so in this regard, it is better than other zero calorie beverages, many of which are loaded with artificial ingredients.

With the taste, Alta Palla Grapefruit Sparkling Water isn’t too bad. It has citrus flavor and the bubbly carbonation keeps you refreshed as you sip. The flavor isn’t sweet, but it isn’t as unsweet/dry as expected either. It’s a little on the bland side, but still not bad when you consider everything.

I like a good, refreshing beverage that doesn’t add inches to the waistline. Alta Palla Grapefruit Sparkling Water is good for this reason. It isn’t the most incredible tasting drink I have ever tried, but it has enough positive points to make it worth a try.

Those who are watching their weight often turn to the keto diet to help reach their goals. These diets have been shown to be quite effective and manufacturers have responded by offering a nice variety of keto- friendly foods and snacks. One that I have tried and found decent is Fat Snax Blondie Bites.

This snack is small and low in calories, with no sugar and minimal carbs. They easily pass the keto test for carb standards and you can eat several of these without feeling guilty. Because they are so small, you likely will want more than one. I try to pack at least two with my lunch, because one is just not enough.

Where these snacks lose some points with me is with the taste. Rich, sugary brownies and cookies are easily tastier than Blondie Bites. If you did a side by side comparison, with one of the better sweet snacks pitted against this one, it would be no contest as to which one tastes better. Then again, these snacks are meant to be different and need to be judged as such.

Avoiding sugar and calories is a good idea for everyone, even if you’re not on a keto diet. Fat Snax Blondie Bites are a good way to avoid the sugar, carbs, and calories and while not as rich and flavorful as other, similar snacks, they are still good enough to recommend and it’s difficult to argue against them when you look at the nutrition label.

I eat snack bars like granola bars and others practically on a daily basis and I like trying different bars and mixing/matching flavors. I’m open to just about any type of bar, and that was enough to get me to try Luna Rica, a chocolate, coconut, and almond bar.

Luna Rica bars have several good things going for them. They are non- GMO, gluten free, and organic certified, which are three traits that will make them immediately appealing to a specific segment of the population. They have a fair amount of protein and fiber, too, but there is a somewhat high level of sugar per bar.

So, the nutrition seems to check out fine but what about the taste? Here, I am less positive about Luna Rica. I like the texture and I appreciate that they are not very chewy. But the taste is a little too tilted toward the flavor of nuts, with almonds and dates dominating. There isn’t as much coconut flavor as I expected and I would prefer that the chocolate be emphasized more. I notice that some other people have noted the same things in their reviews. I’m someone who prefers chocolate, so any breakfast bar that emphasizes that flavor will win out over others.

These bars are on the small side and can be eaten quickly. I suppose they are acceptable overall, but I don’t rank them among my favorites. I can think of several other breakfast bar and granola bar brands that I like more than these and that contain less sugar. Still, these have enough going for them to earn a middle rating and small recommendation, at least as something worthy of a try.

I love chocolate and I like indulging in the occasional chocolate- flavored snack or dessert. My love of all things chocolate- enhanced led me to sample Snackwell Brownie Bites. I like other Snackwell products and there was no reason to think that this one would be any different from the others.

Snackwell Brownie Bites are sold in small packages with about seven small brownie bits per bag. Each package contains only about 150 calories, which isn't too bad. The individual packages are nice because they are just right for an individual treat and they lessen the chances for overindulgence. Open a very large package of a sweet snack and you may continue snacking until your stomach cries out in pain, but open a small pack and there is a better chance you will end your snacking experience when the package is empty.

Smaller packages and servings are certainly nice, but what about the taste and quality of the product? Well, I'm afraid that Snackwell loses a few points in this area. The brownies are a tad on the dry side- nothing like the moist, gooey fudge brownies I had in mind when I first tried them. They are a little chewier than I prefer, too, and in many ways they remind me of a Little Debbie brownie snack. Little Debbie snacks, as most will agree, are acceptable but far from great. And this is exactly how I feel about Snackwell Brownie Bites.

Everyone needs a chocolate fix every now and then and Snackwell Brownie Bites are fine for this purpose. But they are not as tasty as other Snackwell products and they are not even close to the delicious chocolate burst so common with the better brownies. Still, they aren't bad for a quick snack and with only 150 calories, you can indulge yourself without any guilt.

I like trying a variety of different things for my daily lunch. I prefer to go for convenience and more often than not, I bring food with me and eat at my desk. My love of Indian food was enough to convince me to give Saffron Road Chicken Biryani a try.

I prep my frozen meals in the microwave and consume them in full immediately. With this meal, I found it was adequate for a quick lunch, but it lacks in many ways. The spice is only slight. I was expecting to taste more from the caramelized onions. The Basmati rice forms the bulk of what you get and it’s not bad, but you need more than rice to make a good Indian meal.

Possibly the most disappointing aspect of this food is the dryness. It begs to have more sauce added and if I had a bottle of something handy, I would immediately add it. There is some sauce mixed into the meal, but not nearly enough.

Nutritionally, I like that the calories are reasonable, and you do get a nice amount of protein. The sodium isn’t too bad, especially when compared to other frozen meals. These are certainly good points, but the overall flavor and the lack of sufficient sauce convinces me to rate this frozen meal average overall.

I try to avoid snacking. It’s often a bad habit to get into, but there are still those times when a snack is necessary and one I have tried and find to my general liking is Handfulls Crrrunch Bites, Ranch Almonds.

Consisting of small almonds wrapped in a shell, this snack food is a little different from others of its type. Yes, you get the nutty taste of almonds but you also get the rice cracker shell along with a slightly spicy seasoning. The seasoning is like that of ranch dressing with added onion and garlic and I admit that I wasn’t so sure I liked this flavor combo at first. However, it did grow on me with each passing handful.

Nutritionally, this snack isn’t too bad. The sugar content is low and the sodium is kept under control. They seem a little saltier than the facts indicate, but that is because of the seasoning. There isn’t as much salt as you think. They are gluten free, too.

Handfulls Crrrunch Bites, Ranch Almonds are crunchy, but not tiring to eat. They are more fun than the typical bag of nuts and offer a good flavor and snack experience overall. I’m still not completely sold on the taste, but another bag should do the trick!

Most people drink their coffee from Kcups. I, too, usually reach for the Kcups when I’m in the office because they are convenient and everyone can drink what they like. But at home, I use a coffee grinder so I was anxious to try whole bean Twin Peaks Coffee Columbian Decaf.

Free from caffeine, this whole- bean coffee is non- GMO verified and all natural. I don’t mind the elimination of caffeine because, well, it’s a good idea to cut caffeine consumption whenever possible and I already consume a good deal of caffeine in my daytime coffee and tea drinking routine.

These are good qualities, but what matters most is the flavor. And in that regard, well, Twin Peaks isn’t among the richer decaf coffees I have tried. It holds promise at first. The beans look good and the aroma is pleasant. But when you taste it, you notice right away that it is lacking in overall flavor and complexity. There is no bitter edge, which is good, but the flavor is so- so at best.

It's good to go decaf every so often. Twin peaks Coffee Columbian Decaf is an acceptable coffee, but not among the best I have tried. Buy it only if you prefer a milder flavor.

Have been using Xanthan Gum for a couple of years (after discovering how much cheaper it was, because I had to use so much less, than the Thick-It 2 food thickener.  Unfortunately, my kid developed something that is shortened into EoE, and the three triggers are dairy, nuts, xanthan gum.  I had really hoped that Arrowroot Powder would have the thickening capabilities of Xanthan Gum, but it sadly does not...not by a mile.  I would guess this is more consistent with using plain corn starch.  By the time I used enough to barely come close to the thickness we needed (stiff pudding), you could taste the Arrowroot Powder and were also aware of the chalky texture from needing so much Arrowroot Powder.  Also, as my kid is a heavy drooler, after a few bites, if any of his (slobber) was on the spoon and re-entered the bowl, the Arrowroot thickness would disappear.  I can't recommend this for medically necessary thickening (for poor swallowers), but it might be fine for baking or what have you.

It does not.  It has the stuff that's in Splenda - a product not without its own controversies.  Had this contained stevia or monk or agave, then I could see where they could call it BETTER - even though it doesn't set up better than the other stuff and it doesn't taste better than the other stuff (sucralose is yuckier tasting that the aspartame, in my opinion).  So my review is they either need to change the mis-leading name or look into truly making a BETTER sugar free pudding mix.

I try to keep snacking to a minimum and when I do snack, I try my best to go for foods that are healthier than most. And a snack that does measure up well in terms of natural qualities is this Peeled Snacks Organic Apple Clusters with Cinnamon.

This snack food doesn’t contain the lengthy list of chemical additives like some snacks, and this is easily its strongest selling point. The snack is made with just three ingredients: apples, apple juice concentrate, and cinnamon. Plus, it’s organic certified so it easily earns points for purity.

The texture of this snack is crunchy and puffy, so it is lighter than expected. The taste is about what you would expect, with light cinnamon and apple at the forefront of each bite. The taste isn’t very intense, however, and that fact could make this snack a letdown for some. It’s milder in taste than other snacks, but it still goes well with a packed lunch and it’s good for those times when you’re on the run and need a quick bite.

Nutritional figures for this snack are not too bad. There is a little more sugar than I would prefer but you do get a couple grams of fiber per serving. Just remember to multiply the calories and other nutritional data by four if you consume the full package, since there are four servings per package. If you’re a habitual snacker, you won’t have much trouble downing the full contents.

Snacks are best when they at least try to be heathy. Peeled Snacks Organic Apple Clusters Cinnamon is a good snack overall. It may not be the greatest tasting snack you have ever tried, but the organic factor, small list of ingredients, and fiber make this snack deserving of an occasional place in your lunch box.

Drinking water is important, but many among us do not drink as much as we should. One reason is the lack of taste and this is where Mio Liquid Water Enhancer Peach Tea flavor can make a difference. It offers a convenient, quick means to add a little flavor to otherwise bland water, increasing the chances that we will drink as much water as we should.

I have seen and heard about these Mio products for some time, but this one, the peach flavor, is the first one I have ever tried. And now that the experience is behind me, I must admit that my feelings are mixed. Yes, it is better tasting than a plain glass of water and it does encourage me to drink more water on a regular basis. But the flavor is a tad too sweet and there is actually very little peach or tea taste. The overall flavor profile is artificial and not nearly as good as the flavor of a fresh, real, fruity drink.

Along with the taste, another issue I have with this product is the odd smell. It is chemical- like in nature and very unappetizing. I would never guess, by smell alone, that this was a peach flavored liquid. It has an artificial smell that makes you hesitate to try it. I can assure you that it tastes better than it smells once it is diluted in water, but the smell is still unappealing.

There is no nutritional value with this drink, but also no calories so it won't expand the waistline. I recommend not looking at the ingredient list because it will raise many concerns due to the artificial colors and preservatives. These are certainly not good qualities, but on the other hand, most of the beverages on the store shelves are no better and some would say they are even worse. At least with a product like this, you are actually consuming a very small amount of the liquid with each serving, so the negative effects are minimal.

Taking everything into account, Mio Liquid Water Enhancer Peach Tea flavor is a tough product to rate. The taste is okay, but nothing amazing. The ingredient list raises some eyebrows, but is no worse than many other common beverages. There is no nutritional value, but also no calories. On the positive side, the product is packaged in a small container, is very convenient, and encourages consumption of water. So, with all of these good and bad qualities, I am going to take the take the middle ground and give this beverage flavoring a middle rating overall. It isn't anything special, but it has enough going for it to consider a sample.

I eat cereal regularly and I have since I was a kid. I like almost any cereal and I’m generally not picky about brands, flavor, etc. I like to consume healthier options and that desire led me to try this Puffed Quinoa Cereal from Wholefort.

First, let’s talk packaging. It is different from other cereal. It comes in a pouch that you can reseal. This is better than traditional boxed cereals that require you to fold the inner packaging, to make it airtight. I feel better with this type of packaging because the built- in seal is more secure.

There are only three ingredients in this cereal, which is a definite plus. But it also means there is less overall nutrition with this cereal compared to other, fortified cereals. There is a good deal of protein, however, so it does have its advantages. And with added milk, you still get a good deal of nutrition overall.

So, most importantly, what about the taste? Well, this cereal is natural in flavor but I must admit it lacks the tasty appeal of most other cereals. And I don’t mean just the sugary- sweet cereals or chocolatey cereals or other enhanced flavors. Even the other, natural cereals I consume have a better overall taste that this one.

It’s great to eat healthier foods. I love cereal and Puffed Quinoa cereal is a decent option overall. I like the packaging and small ingredient list, but taste- wise, I like other natural cereals better.

Quaker Chewy Dipps peanut butter bars are a tasty snack that is sure to satisfy anyone who loves the flavors of granola and chocolate. This snack food product takes an ordinary granola bar and coats it with a layer of milk chocolate to create a snack that is more chocolaty and sweeter than the average granola bar.

I eat granola bars almost every day and Quaker Dipps are certainly high- ranking in the taste department. Granola with added milk chocolate- who wouldn't love such a combination? However, as much as I love the taste of Quaker Chew Dipps, I cannot overlook some of the negative qualities of these granola bars. First, there are some questionable ingredients such as high fructose corn syrup, cottonseed oil, artificial colors, etc. Other granola bar brands and flavors contain similar less- than- healthy ingredients, but their lists are not as long as the list for Quaker Chew Dipps. The ingredient list for this product includes multiple lines, which cannot be a good thing. Second, these granola bars are quite small. Other granola bars are larger and/or chewier and take more time to eat. These bars are only slightly chewy and their small size makes them disappear in seconds.

The added milk chocolate on a Quaker Dipps bar makes them seem like candy bars. And compared to a candy bar, Quaker Chewy Dipps are certainly better. They do contain some wholesome ingredients and actually make a good choice when you have a sweet craving and need a fix. There are many questionable ingredients and the nutritional content is nothing to brag about, but at least you get a little bit of nutritional value and it is still better than that of most candy bars.

Granola is a favorite snack food and Quaker Chewy Dipps are a good tasting snack that rate highly based on taste but much lower based on ingredients, nutrition, size, and other factors. I consider them an average snack overall and while I certainly won't turn one away, they include enough negative qualities that I don't seek these out when I shop. They taste great, but the negative qualities mentioned above are enough to make them average overall.
