There's a risk that you take when you want to watch a lesser known film; it could be a diamond in the rough, or just rough. Prozac Nation could double as both the title of the film and a description of those who've seen it, because most would be tempted to pop a pill afterwards to reduce their irritation after watching this seemingly unfinished film that can't decide whether it's an advertisement for pharmaceuticals or a warning against them.

Because this film is based on Elizabeth Wurtzel's autobiography, a book I admittedly have not read, I can't presume to know how true to form is Ricci's portrayal of Wurtzel. That being said, Ricci's Wurtzel is the Queen of Harvard Brats, part caricature and part zombie, a woman who, in full recognition of her issues with depression, would rather use her condition as a weapon to manipulate those around her, as opposed to putting that energy into healing herself.

Throughout the film I couldn't help but wonder why Wurtzel's story, above others, was important enough to turn into a film. Prozac Nation is filled with nothing more than recycled tropes and clunky editing.

Michelle Williams' Ruby is basically Jen Lindley: College Edition (her character from Dawson's Creek, which was still filming at the time, so it wasn't much of an acting stretch). At one point, Lizzie is being more of a sniveling bitch to Ruby than usual and Ruby cries. When Lizzie spites Ruby for crying, Ruby defends herself, saying that she isn't upset because Lizzie is mean, she's upset because Lizzie has to essentially wake up every day as Lizzie. At first I thought this was ridiculous - who could actually cry because their "friend" lived such a miserable life? But I think I too would be depressed to realize that there is nothing redeeming about someone I called a friend, in part because what would that say, then, about me?

The only thing Lizzie supposedly has going for her is her writing talent (she wins an award for it from Rolling Stone). We hear all the time about how she's either writing or not writing, and we realize that this is one of those films that reminds you endlessly without saying outright that its source material is a novel. But Ricci's passionless voice-overs convey pretty standard fare, especially for a Harvard student.

Jason Biggs plays Rafe, the dude Lizzie bumps into while in an ecstasy-induced haze in the men's bathroom. Rafe is a typical Jason Biggs character so, again, not much of an acting stretch. Rafe seems nice enough, so of course you know he's going to hook up with Lizzie after she makes a series of mistakes first (Jonathan Rhys Meyers' stoner, Noah, is one of those mistakes).

Rafe and Lizzie's relationship proceeds so fast that even the film can't keep up. They barely know each other when they say "I love you," and Lizzie's already making plans to take him home to meet Mom for the holidays. We generally get excited to bring our significant others home for the holidays because holidays are when we spend time with family. So bringing someone home essentially makes him or her part of our family, right? So you would think that Lizzie finds family to be important, which is why she's so psyched to bring Rafe home to hers.

Except that when Rafe informs her that he can't go home with her because he is needed with his own family, Lizzie pitches yet another fit (which we are bored with by this point) because it's yet another instance where she was mistaken in thinking that the world orbits around her.

And just when you think that Lizzie can't be any more detestable, she goes off the deep end, accusing Rafe of "getting off on" taking care of his special needs sister - to what end, I have no idea, and I don't think she does either. I was amazed that Rafe handled the accusation as calmly as he did, because this was one of those plentiful moments in the film when Lizzie deserved a slap across the mouth, Real Housewives-style.

There is not one likeable character in this film, though the only one who isn't walking around in shades of gray is Jessica Lange as Lizzie's mother. Unfortunately, Lange was only given two modes here in which to operate: infuriated or defeated. This would be a great role for an actress who needed a release from a painful situation like a death or divorce because all Lange does is scream and cry. She must have been exhausted when her scenes were wrapped. As for her character, the poor woman has experienced her husband leaving her, an insufferable brat for a child, and a violent mugging. If I was her, I'd leave town without leaving a forwarding address.

Questions have been posed regarding Ricci's nudity at the beginning of the film. While vulnerability is usually the go-to explanation, that's just hard to swallow as far as Lizzie is concerned. I'm wont to agree with the explanation that Lizzie just couldn't be bothered to get dressed. Seems apropos in accordance with her personality.

My main issue with Lizzie is that she takes the first step - she admits she has a problem - but that's as far as it goes. Sure, she sees a therapist, Dr. Sterling (Anne Heche), whom her mother pays, but Lizzie spends most of the sessions fighting the treatment she's receiving rather than being open to it. She wants everyone else to fix her, and she charges Rafe with "saving her life," rather than taking some responsibility and owning up to her mistakes like an adult.

Lizzie's last scene with Dr. Sterling involves the former trying to commit suicide in what we come to realize is the latter's house. And how do we realize this? Because, of course, Dr. Sterling's daughter comes toddling in to witness it. Dr. Sterling then proceeds to pick up her daughter and embark on a staring contest with Lizzie in one of the most pregnant pauses in the history of cinema that conjures up memories of Weird Al's parody of R. Kelly's opus, Trapped in the Closet: "And she looks at me. And I look at her. And she looks at me! And I look at her!" The film ends with Lizzie accepting her prescription from the local drug store and standing there watching creepily as others do the same.

Up pops a title card before the credits that reads "In the United States alone, more than 300 million prescriptions are filled every year for Prozac and other anti-depressants." That's it. That's all she wrote. I guess literally.

It's impossible to tell if this film's message is for anti-depressants or against them. The way that card is worded, one would think they were condemning "Big Pharma," yet Lizzie finally accepts that she needs meds when she nearly kills herself. She also admitted earlier that she was able to better focus on her writing with the help of medication. It seems like Prozac Nation wants to take a side, but it's afraid it will piss people off, which is truly ironic.

Notable:

- Viewers have asked "how can they reference Facebook when this film was made in 2001 (and takes place in the '80s)?" They're not referencing Mark Zuckerberg's brainchild; they're referring to the school's yearbook. This just goes to show how deeply the term "Facebook" has penetrated our lexicon, when a "face book" can no longer exist as anything other than a digital medium.

- Wurtzel's dad looks a lot like Lou Reed, the rock musician that hipster Lizzie admires. Coincidence? Or Daddy issues?

- What is with Lizzie's father circling around her like a carousel during their argument and the camera following suit. Perhaps they were aiming for dramatic tension here, but the scene isn't even scored, so it becomes unintentionally laughable.

The SM7b has been my dream mic and I was so happy to finally get it. However, if you are just switching from USB to XLR mics for the first time like me, it is a lot more of a journey/expense than you may think!

It has a beautiful build and the most attractive mic out there by far. Very boomy and I have a deeper voice for a woman so I did sound pretty nice with it. However, to fully make use of it you need to have a Cloudlifter as the SM7b is notorious for having extremely low gain.

Materials needed/What I got:

For basics you need a sound mixer, XLR cable, and optional but highly recommended, a cloudlifter.

I got the Elgato Wave XLR (rn $159.99) but for the XLR cable a high quality one is recommended. Cloudlifter is $149 rn and the SM7b is $399.99. A popular mixer that is also used is the Focusrite ($179 rn).

Therefore $159.99 (mixer) + $399.99 (sm7b) + $149 (cl) = $708.98+ starter price not even including the XLR cable.

So keep this in mind if you plan on using this and that you are ready to jump into this investment. It is worth it if you have the money/resources needed!

I'm an elementary band director and I've had several students show up with these instruments. Please trust me - I've probably had 20 kids with Mendinis and have a lot more experience than one parent who (at the time of a review) had a positive outlook. Band directors all cringe at the name of Mendini, or any other of the cheap Amazon instruments. They. Do. Not. Last.

1. Really, they do not last. I've never had a student play on one for more than a year. They break all the time. Constantly. Weekly, sometimes. Because they are cheap, noname instruments, they can't be fixed by repair shops because replacement parts don't exist and/or don't work. Many repair shops refuse to even try to fix it because even if it could be fixed, the metal is so thin, cheap, and brittle that it will either break or bend right back. Kids get frustrated when their instrument is never working and then quit.

2. The tone is horrible. Absolutely horrible. This is not me being a picky band director - the kids even notice it and point it out. Nobody wants their kid to be singled out because they are doing some poorly - and it's not even the kid's fault!

3. The tuning is extremely off. This is noticeable by the kids as well. It's to the point where the instrument is playing a completely different note than the one that it is supposed to sound like. No amount of adjustment from the kid or teacher will fix this. Your child will stick out and be embarrassed.

4. No resale value. The instrument will not last long enough to even have value after a few months, but even if it did, it will not hold value. Good instruments are atill valuable years after - people are still playing on instruments from the 40s. In a few months, you will be stuck with a perpetually broken instrument that either you will pour money in to trying to fix it, or that is so broken it can't be fixed and then you are flat out of that money.

I understand; band is expensive. But you could take this money and find a used instrument - a good brand- and end up with something that will WORK! If that's not an option, talk to your school's band director to see if they have school instruments available.

Trust me - this is NOT band director approved. We call Mendinis 'instrument shaped objects' because that is exactly all they are.

If you ignore the rest of my advice, at least do your kid one favor - don't get them a weird color of this instrument. It will only help all the other kids identify whose sound is bad even faster.

These reeds are utter crap! I've used Rico #2 reeds for a number of years because of their ease of use and that you don't have to blow too hard for them to work correctly. I went through this entire 3 pack in one concert and a rehearsal a few day later.

After I got a convertible standing desk riser, I needed something to sit beside my desk to hold my laptop as there isn’t much room left on my desk anymore. I needed it to be adjustable in height so I can lift it up higher when I use the standing position and lower it back down when I sit to work.

This was fairly easy to put together. It only took me about 7 minutes to fully assemble. What I noticed right away is how “sharp” the edges are, like it lacked finishing or was just cheaply made. That became more apparent as I screwed on the knobs. The plastic and metal pieces all have sharp edges that could have been refined better, but it’s not super major. They’re just not nice to touch and the knobs are a bit hard to twist because they’re poking into your hand the whole time. And the tripod base is made of very thin metal, so I definitely wouldn’t use this outdoors or anywhere the floor isn’t level and there’s no danger of big gusts or other environmental risk of it being knocked over.

I also wouldn’t plan on toting this around. If you intend to carry it in the bag it came with, you’d have to break everything completely down and I’m not sure the tray part even fits in that bag. And you’d have to take the tray off the top extension tube, which would require unscrewing those four screws every time. Not a great design for portability.

The foam piece is super thin and had a big gouge or manufacturing crease in the middle. If you were counting on the foam cradling an expensive projector or other piece of equipment, I’d suggest buying another piece of foam and cutting it to size.

It works for my needs, but I don’t plan to move this around or use it for anything other than my laptop. Overall it’s just okay in my opinion. Nothing terrible, nothing great. Three solid stars.

2. The tone is horrible. Absolutely horrible. This is not me being a picky band director - the kids even notice it and point it out. Nobody wants their kid to be singled out because they are doing some poorly - and it's not even the kid's fault!

3. The tuning is extremely off. This is noticeable by the kids as well. It's to the point where the instrument is playing a completely different note than the one that it is supposed to sound like. No amount of adjustment from the kid or teacher will fix this. Your child will stick out and be embarrassed.

4. No resale value. The instrument will not last long enough to even have value after a few months, but even if it did, it will not hold value. Good instruments are atill valuable years after - people are still playing on instruments from the 40s. In a few months, you will be stuck with a perpetually broken instrument that either you will pour money in to trying to fix it, or that is so broken it can't be fixed and then you are flat out of that money.

I understand; band is expensive. But you could take this money and find a used instrument - a good brand- and end up with something that will WORK! If that's not an option, talk to your school's band director to see if they have school instruments available.

Trust me - this is NOT band director approved. We call Mendinis 'instrument shaped objects' because that is exactly all they are.

If you ignore the rest of my advice, at least do your kid one favor - don't get them a weird color of this instrument. It will only help all the other kids identify whose sound is bad even faster.

First of all, even though the description says this microphone is a "studio professional" mic, don't compare it to a "real" studio professional microphone!! For an inexpensive alternative, it does fine. The audio quality is actually pretty decent on this mic but becuz there is virtually no compression, it picks up everything. If you are going to use this for speaking and want to do things like Youtube videos, etc, you will need a pop filter so it doesn't pick up all the background noise and ambient noise. It is easy plug and play - you do not need a phantom power source and that is perfect for someone that just wants to do an easy in home set up. The stand is rather clumsy and falls apart easily - again, not something that is critical to its function, but rather an annoyance and the jack that plugs in seemed a little loose on mine. It did work, but just did not seem like a tight fit like it should have been. This mic works for my purpose and after all, you are not out an arm and a leg on price. I received this product for free/discount in exchange for a fair and honest review. All opinions are completely my own and if this review was helpful to you, please hit the "yes" button at the end of the review!

I really wanted to love this wooden recorder, but after many attempts to achieve an acceptable, consistent sound quality from it, I decided to return it.

I purchased this three-piece recorder with baroque fingering and half holes. It’s made of solid maple wood with a beautiful finish. It appears to be well made; however, the overall sound consistency was flawed, making it frustrating to play. I’m new to the recorder, but not new to woodwind instruments. I played the closed hole, transverse flute for many years in a large orchestra setting as first chair. So I’m experienced with the importance of achieving a flexible embouchure for sound quality. Effective air flow on the recorder is much easier to achieve, and breathing from the diaphragm has become natural to me. So I honestly don’t think my air flow was the problem. I know it takes time to properly “break-in” a wooden instrument; but by then, it would be too late to return it. So I opted to focus my need to make music on a penny whistle, as well as an alto recorder from Yamaha, both of which I bought here on Amazon. I just want to learn something new to play now that I’m retired!

I'm an elementary band director and I've had several students show up with these instruments. Please trust me - I've probably had 20 kids with Mendinis and have a lot more experience than one parent who (at the time of a review) had a positive outlook. Band directors all cringe at the name of Mendini, or any other of the cheap Amazon instruments. They. Do. Not. Last.

1. Really, they do not last. I've never had a student play on one for more than a year. They break all the time. Constantly. Weekly, sometimes. Because they are cheap, noname instruments, they can't be fixed by repair shops because replacement parts don't exist and/or don't work. Many repair shops refuse to even try to fix it because even if it could be fixed, the metal is so thin, cheap, and brittle that it will either break or bend right back. Kids get frustrated when their instrument is never working and then quit.

2. The tone is horrible. Absolutely horrible. This is not me being a picky band director - the kids even notice it and point it out. Nobody wants their kid to be singled out because they are doing some poorly - and it's not even the kid's fault!

3. The tuning is extremely off. This is noticeable by the kids as well. It's to the point where the instrument is playing a completely different note than the one that it is supposed to sound like. No amount of adjustment from the kid or teacher will fix this. Your child will stick out and be embarrassed.

4. No resale value. The instrument will not last long enough to even have value after a few months, but even if it did, it will not hold value. Good instruments are atill valuable years after - people are still playing on instruments from the 40s. In a few months, you will be stuck with a perpetually broken instrument that either you will pour money in to trying to fix it, or that is so broken it can't be fixed and then you are flat out of that money.

I understand; band is expensive. But you could take this money and find a used instrument - a good brand- and end up with something that will WORK! If that's not an option, talk to your school's band director to see if they have school instruments available.

Trust me - this is NOT band director approved. We call Mendinis 'instrument shaped objects' because that is exactly all they are.

This amp is amazing, & the presets are honestly super cool. Looks nice & sounds great. Plus the bag it comes with is SUPER handy. I’ve only had 2 issues thus far that could be isolated to just my unit, or is a bug that can be patched in future updates.

[1st ISSUE]:
Every single time I turn this amp on & want to use the app. I have to wait 5min for it to connect if I just wait. I figured out a workaround to waiting 5min+ (not exaggerating) for the app to find the speaker; here’s my process:

1) turn speaker on
2) press “connect” on the app
3) press “retry” after it says it “failed to connect”
4) press “cancel” because it’ll take 5min otherwise
5) press “connect,” after 10 seconds, it finally connects.

Hopefully this helps anyone else who has this issue, because it took me a few weeks to figure it out.

[2nd ISSUE]:
The tuner is unreliable. It had me tune my A string crazy low, so I had to whip out a tuning app on my phone, which told me it was -9. Needless to say, I’m disappointed a free app is better than the tuner in this $300 amp

For reference:
• The Spark app is updated to latest version
• I tried the app on my iPhone 11 Pro Max & iPad Air 4th gen, both using the latest version of iOS
• BlueTooth on my iPhone & iPad work just fine (no other devices like AirPods, keyboards, or anything else have this issue)
• I’ve updated the firmware on the amp itself by following the video tutorial Spark posted on YouTube.
• The app takes forever to connect 100% of the time if I don’t intervene.

Otherwise I love this amp & I can’t imagine a better one for this price. If it wasn’t for the app connection being a huge task every time, I’d give this 6 stars. Honestly now that I’m used to it, it’s not so bad, but when I first got it, I was furious.

I bought two of these for my daughters, age 4 and 6, so they could each have one to play. So I want to start off by saying both instruments were pretty and sounded very nice. Everything came as described and pictured. I thought they were very reasonably priced for having a hard carrying case.
Here was my issue and reason for returning. In the accessory pouch (a little mesh drawstring bag) was a thick cardboard thank you card saturated with perfume. So everything smells like perfume (instrument, carrying case, all the accessories) and since the perfume card touched everything in the case everything has perfume on it. Not only did it give me a headache (not a good smelling perfume and way too much) but I was concerned as I bought to give to my daughters and my oldest daughter has moderate to severe asthma and I really think it would cause an asthma attack. To put it perspective the amount of perfume involved...I removed the perfume cardboard pieces and sat them aside, washed my hands twice, and I still have the perfume smell on my hands. I do not believe it will air out and I do not believe it will come off the cleaning cloth or the liner of the carrying case being that they're fabric and I'm not so sure the smell would come off the instrument either. So I am returning them and ordering another brand which hopefully does not have a perfume cardboard thank you card.

It's been many years since I've sold on eBay, but do occasionally need to send a package or large envelope and a receipt.  For those times, the Avery shipping label with receipt all on one single page is a convenient supply to have on hand. She finally gave up on it when it did it again a month or so later.  So I tried that --- it still does not work.  I would not recommend this product to anyone.  That was $95 down the drain.

Mont Blanc doesn't offer a huge selection of ink colors, so if you're determined to get a red MB ink, the [[ASIN:B008SBO7CK Montblanc Burgundy Red New Ink Cartridge 2010 Refill]] is your choice.  It's also available in the lovely Mont Blanc bottle, [[ASIN:B003M5T3XA Genuine Montblanc Bottled Ink Refill - Burgundy Red 105198]], which is a better value if your pen has a converter or you're comfortable performing the simple task of refilling your cartridge.  I was expecting the color to be as described, "For expression Burgundy Red blends rounded wine tones with flaming fire for intense passionate language."  In reality, when I compared this ink in my [[ASIN:B00C4180LO Mont Blanc Meisterstuck Mozart Platinum Line Mini Fountain Pen 107706]] and a [[ASIN:B00EVC948C Pilot MR Animal Collection Fountain Pen, Matte Gold with Lizard Accent, Medium Point, 91136]] containing [[ASIN:B0013D2XYY Private Reserve Black Cherry Ink Bottle 50 ML]], my impression was that the color is nearly as brown as the PR.  It is a little more red, but there's nto a huge difference between the two.  Nevertheless, the cartridges work as expected in Mont Blanc fountain pens.  I purchased mine in a pen store for a substantial savings over the current prices.

The shape of this eraser fits more comfortably in the hand than a traditional blocky style of chalkboard or whiteboard eraser.  Much more comfortably,  and the ends of it can be used in crevices with ease,  so I can have better access into areas inside the edge of a frame.  With two different surfaces on one removable section,  I can use the one I need,  then reverse it when I need the other side.  There is also a section with a piece of blue microfiber fabric which we all are familiar with,  the kind we use for washing,  drying and polishing our cars,  homes, boats and pet's faces and paws. Keep the old ratty ones with holes or stains because they can be cut to the shape of this eraser and stuck to the textured area under the existing microfiber.  I expect the one included to eventually ravel away at the pointed ends because of their narrow form.  This is a very smart,  space saving little tool for home or work.

Made in China, the [[ASIN:B00KSEAID2 Mead Academie Watercolor Paint Set, 12 Pans, Assorted (98024)]] comes in a white metal tin case and is a good size for portability.  Each of the dozen colors gives an immediate bland impression when compared to other paints geared to adults, but for transparents, they are adequate.  Nicer than the cheaper Prang paints we used when I taught Middle School Art, I would consider these a step or two up the ladder.  Students might experiment with cosmetics and make-up initially, while as they mature, they likely will discern characteristics and qualities as they gain experience.  The same holds true for artists and their colors.  Because these are translucent, the colors are not vibrant or rich, but they can be blended with others to create lovely effects.  The included brush is very nice although the one in my case is a bit warped.  If I were getting this for a new artist, I'd be certain to include other brushes because this has a fine tip and I think they could benefit from broader versions as well.  The brush is very soft.  It is a nice set of water-soluble watercolours [sic].

Made in the USA, the [[ASIN:B00D8GK904 Intertape Polymer Group TS2650 Bundle Wrap Stretch Film, 2-Inch x 650-Feet]] is certainly expensive, but it definitely sticks to itself, just like it's supposed to.  It is wonderful for bundling items for mailing or storing, but it is also perfect for stretching around a bottle top, sealing it to itself for security without adhesive.  It's not for a full seal or pretty presentation, but it is an added security if you are shipping things like a bottle of homemade vanilla extract and you want to provide an extra level of leakage protection. In my experience, any dry erase markers do not last long, they tend to dry out if not used often.  I've also found that most have been of the low odor ink formula, and although I don't know for a fact, perhaps this is a reason they do not remain moist.  Nevertheless, I really like these pens for several reasons.  The rubberized grip is definitely easy on the fingers and if my hand is wet with water or moisturizer, I can keep a hold of the marker.  The point is what is called fine, but don't confuse this with the mush finer tips as you would find in a permanent marker.  The ink is solid, flow is even when the pen is new, the cap stays on when snapped on with a little pressure, and I've been able to rig up a hanger attached to the pocket clip of the cap which I've hung next to the mirror at our front door.  I can write directly on the glass face of the mirror and erase it with a tissue or fabric as required.  It's a good option for those who need to write on white boards, glass or mirrors, and I appreciate that they were made in the USA, even if some of the materials have been outsourced.

The AmazonBasics 17-Sheet Cross-Cut Paper/CD/Credit Card Shredder with Pullout Basket is a workhorse of a shredder and it weighs a ton.  Fortunately, it arrives with attachable rolling wheels (be sure you insert the locking pair in the front of the unit) because it otherwise would be better suited for a designated spot.  On the wheels, it can be moved from room to room as needed, so if you have the need for mobility, this is for you.  The machine is very intuitive and the manual is quite simple to follow, too.  I first shredded an old users manual but fed it into the shredder vertically, so I was able to read the little slices of paper in the bin.  This did stress to me the importance of feeding the sheets through just as they are read, thereby resulting in slices which are more difficult to reassemble.  I have another shredder which produces little confetti pieces, but that one has always been a struggle to operate. These strips are longer, but the machine works much better.  It is suggested to use it for no longer than nine minutes at a time, but since I seldom need to do that much shredding, it's not an issue here.  Of important note is that when shredding CDs or credit cards, be sure to insert them in the right side of the shredder.  It is clearly marked, but I know what it's like when I'm on auto pilot and don't think or observe, just do.  With a very large basket, it's not necessary to empty the paper as often as in smaller models which would likely compete in this price range.  I insert a bag in mine so that when the time comes, I have little to clean out.  Most does remain in the bag.  The manual makes no recommendation to oil the shredder, but since I have been in the habit of routinely oiling my previous models, I'll likely do the same with this one unless I learn that it's not suggested.  All in all, I love this shredder.

The Rhodia Notepads Graph Black Wb 8.3 x 11.7 is a dream to write on.  The large dimensions allow it to be held comfortably on the lap, so a table or desk aren't required as often is the case with smaller pads of paper.  The paper is white, the grids are gray, and they are perfectly printed.  With micro perforations at the top of the page, removal is simple.  I just received two pads and both are interesting in that the wire binding on both pads is finished with all four ends of the wire is turned in toward the center of the page.  Imagine that they have been pushed inward so as to secure the spiral wires.  I didn't notice this in the pads I checked out at a pen store prior to my purchase, but both of these pads share the characteristic, so there you have it.  I have used mechanical pencil, rollerball, fiber tip and fountain pens on the paper and each works well.  Because the paper is so smooth and substantial in weight, even with a fountain pen using dark black ink, I am able to flip a sheet over and continue writing on the reverse side.  Therefore, on an 80 sheet pad, I have 160 pages which to cover with ink or graphite.  I prefer the wire bound pads to the stapled pads because of the ease with which I can have a single sheet visible and I find the pad to be sturdy enough to require no other backing.  Fountain pen nibs move more smoothly on this paper than most others I've used lately, and I am reminded why I first bought a pad of Rhodia paper in France many, many years ago.  The graph option is my favorite but I'm likely to try the dot next.  My only criticism of purchasing this from Amazon is that a sticker is applied to the back of the pad and it's annoying to work to fully remove the residue. It took a lot of rubbing because I didn't want to apply anything even though the covers are water resistant.  Be aware that your pad of paper will likely not remain pristine for long.  These are made to be filled and that means they will be touched, often moved from place to place.  They don't hold up like a bound journal will because they are simple bound sheets of paper in a thin front and back cover with a cardboard sheet inserted at the back of the sheets of paper for support.

My first impression of the [[ASIN:B00GDDV5UW Mead Organizher School Memory Keeper, 14-Folder Organizher, 10.25 x 11.5 Inches, Purple (64051)]] was unfavorable. It looked cheap and unattractive. I thought that it couldn't possibly hold everything a memory keeper should hold. After looking at it for several days, touching it a few times, today I did a test run by filling the folders with pamphlets and catalogues to see how much it would hold. To my surprise, the binder holds a substantial amount and the elastic band which is bolted into the back cover stretches amply to keep the binder, closing tightly. This binder isn't waterproof or protective at all, but it's fine for keeping things that don't need to be kept indefinitely. We could always keep the binder inside a vacuum sealed bag for long term protection, then take it out annually, I guess, but I find a plastic sealed box to be better. My compromise is that this will be kept inside the plastic box instead of on a shelf. We will digitally retain all things by scanning or photographing, but the tangible items are what we really want for memories. Of the sections designated on the back of each folder, I think my favorite line is the one for a signature. I would like to see how that changes over fourteen years time. This memory keeper apparently is currently available in a purple or gray, neither of which I think is beautiful nor memorable. I'm not a fan of the Mead name for this line, "Organizher," and had I not thought it was a listing typo, I likely would not have requested this item.

This is better than average but not a fabulous product. I personally prefer using three ringed binders with available sleeves that do the same as the ones in this product. I like the option of removing the pages and adding another binder when necessary and prefer not paying fifteen dollars for a matching binder, if it will still be available in years to come. We tend to keep a lot of memories which we shuffle through over time, so this isn't a great product for our family, but it might be a good product for those who like to maintain a more orderly organizer. My sister likes to keep things like this standardized and displayed on a shelf, so she would have loved these for her three kids. Although the quality isn't as high, this reminds me of leather bound photo albums she had lining a bookshelf for her kids so I may buy her a couple of these for her grandchildren. She won't have as many things to fill the pages and one will be designated for each child. I think it would be better for grandparents than parents who collect a lot of their kids' creations and accolades.

Setting up the[[ASIN:B005IVL0RS Epson WorkForce 845 Wireless Color Printer (C11CB92201)]] was not difficult once I'd downloaded the software onto my Mac running Snow Leopard.  Printing while wired was seamless, fast, quieter than I'd expected for such a large printer, and I anticipated the wireless operation to be just as simple.  The quality of printing was superb for an inkjet printer and I couldn't wait to try it out on a network.  Unfortunately, this task was not as easy but after several attempts and phone calls, I'm finally up and running, quite happy that I can now even print from my smartphone.  Because the included cartridges are full size, I haven't yet ordered replacement inks, but will once again return to Amazon for those purchases.  It's been some years since I owned an Epson printer, but this one is now certainly utilized in my home office, sending the little Canon printer into the closet for emergencies only and putting the Brother MF on hiatus.  The Epson is much easier to operate, produces a better quality print job and has a more legible screen.  I definitely recommend this printer and suggest that if you're using a Mac, don't be discouraged.  It's worth the effort.

With close to a month of usage, the Samsung SL-C1860FW/XAA Wireless Color Printer with Scanner, Copier and Fax is providing a much appreciated upgrade to my home office. Its larger than I'd expected, but I had not researched its size prior to requesting the machine. The box I received was intact and it required two of us to remove the product from the package. Setting it up was problematic at first because there was no CD to be found anywhere. After I'd downloaded the manual from the Samsung website, I finally found the CD hidden inside the box, so if you purchase this and can't find the CD, keep looking. Enclosed in clear sight was a warranty and two folded pages with installation information. I found them to be convoluted, but that could be my problem. I also had difficulty connecting this to my network, and finally was able to connect wirelessly. I honestly do not know the steps I took, but it does work and it's securely a part of my own network. The cartridges are easy and quick to install, as is the paper tray. I was apprehensive with the tray because it was more lightweight than those on the enormous laser printers I used in days past. Visually, this machine is attractive and boxy, neutral in colors and the screen is very intuitive and clear. Although not required per the documentation, I downloaded the Samsung software on our smartphones and we are not using this printer in this manner rather than using Google Cloud. I've not attempted to use the NFC Print via the one smartphone with capabilities only because I haven't needed that yet.

Copying is fast and accurate, crisp and clean. Scans are fantastic and reliable. The fax I tested was successful. Printing was equally successful when sent via USB or WiFi. The option to print configurations is appreciated, as that allows for keeping all information in a folder kept near the printer. I use the Eco mode because I don't use the machine every day. I haven't notice warmth around the machine and it is quiet when not functional. For my home office, the speed of printing is great, and although I likely will not use this for as much dedicated color printing as my other inkjet printers, for documents which include color, this is better than my other printers. The major weakness for my needs is that this does not have duplex printing. I have yet to experience a paper jam. I'm far from needing replacement toners, but hope that their prices are reduced before that day comes. My initial thought regarding the cost of this machine was that it's priced higher than it is worth, but considering the cost of the stuff that goes onto the paper is about the same as this entire package, I guess I won't complain. If you are in the market for a very good color multifunctional printer, this is definitely worth considering.

Having received three of these Avery Durable clear cover binders over the past few weeks, I can no longer remain silent. Each one has a pocket inside the cover and each one has a pocket which has split. The exterior looks good, particularly when personalized sheets of paper have been inserted into the cover sleeves, each one has perfectly functioning slant rings, each one holds the tabbed dividers perfectly, but the interior pockets look as though they were sliced with a knife. These are very fine for home purposes, but I would not consider them for distribution of materials requiring more than occasional use and would hesitate before using them for student purposes which require daily use.

The hardware is excellent in these binders and they are attached well into the rear of the binder so the sturdiness is increased, I just do not like the flimsy plastic parts of the pockets which are terrible.

My only criticism of the Avery full sheets sticker paper in beautiful gold is that the backing could use more slices to allow for quicker and cleaner removal after smaller sections are printed.  I think that most people will cut these either by mechanical or scissor/blade methods, and if you make smaller stickers as I've done, you may find that some must be peeled from the edge.  The paper is thick, so with care, destruction can be avoided, and if you are not a perfectionist, you might not mind anyway.  Since I often have problems with Avery templates printing not so perfectly onto precut shapes, I'm more satisfied by cutting my own with a blade after printing.  I've been having fun with these and appreciate the exposure to a full sized sheet of sticker paper.

When I first received the [[ASIN:B004XOK1SU Scotch Box Sealing Tape Dispenser H122, 2 in]], I was pleased with the recognizable Scotch red plaid box and thought that it was smaller than I'd expected. Using this is a breeze, I just apply pressure to the top (brake) to alter the speed with which the tape unrolls from the dispenser and is applied to the item being taped.  With a metal blade that's replaceable, I expect this to last a long time.  I like this much better than my dispensers that use a single handle and engineering that causes frustration as the tape wildly unrolls and/or sticks to itself.  This is a good product.

I ordered this before beginning my first job as a teacher. I finished grad school in December and was hired for a mid-year opening when another teacher decided to move during the break. My first day was January 8, and I had five classes (high school). I used this book for attendance and for grades. I liked that the cover was not just a solid color, and I liked that the blocks were slightly larger. And you couldn't beat the cost.
But like most things, you get what you pay for, it seems. I took this out of my rolling briefcase each morning and put it back at the end of the day. If I was grading papers at home in the evening, it came out and was replaced before I went to bed. Within three weeks, I was running into my local office supply store hoping to find one of those generic record books with the solid cover because the cover to this was falling off at the plastic coil, and several of the pages were also beginning to separate from the coil. Before I made the complete transfer of data, I had ended up removing the coil altogether and holding the pages together with a trio of small binder clips down the edge where the coil should have been.

No instructions.  No gloves.  Nothing to put underneath the printer. No smock for you to wear.  I paid for ink and I received ink.  To my surprise, removing the seal from the bottle is the most difficult part and I was fortunately already gloved up, doing it at the kitchen sink when I discovered that it wouldn't just peel off.  Paper was already placed underneath the printer, my clothes were impervious to staining, paper towels were next to the printer, and the cap on the tank was lifted up and placed on its holding spot.  (Lift it up, doesn't screw.)  Unscrew the outer cap from the ink bottle and carefully tilt it into the tank, gently squeeze until the ink reaches the fill line.  Lift the bottle back, wipe it off, recap if there's ink remaining.  Place the cap onto the filled tank and check again for messes.  Check everything and wipe up any drips or dribbles.  Be sure to check dark areas. like where the cap was placed and the printer case.  Repeat with other tanks as needed.  Check your clothes.  Remove your gloves.  Reset the ink levels on the printer in Maintenance and test your printer, cleaning the heads if necessary and check alignment.  I've had no problem with the ink printing clearly and will repurchase without a doubt.

After waiting for the delivery of this talking travel translator, I now have to wait for it to actually translate. It arrived intact in a little package without much non-recyclable materials, just the disposable plastic case which is easy to open and not much larger than the actual 3/4 of a deck of cards size of the translator. Included was a sheet of paper with 705 English travel related phrases, and a second sheet of paper which is the schematics and operating manual. Both are in English, folded and double-sided. Now for a review of the product.

PROS:
A voice announces what is on a little screen and you can change the language through any or all 7 options.
With one button, you can hear it in your "Home" language.
Favorites can be saved.
It is small and lightweight.
If you study this, you can learn phrases and speak them yourself, maybe after showing the display to a person who speaks the language you want to learn.
You can enter addresses.

CONS:
If it needs to be reset or batteries fail, you lose all your addresses and saved favorites and there is no way to backup.
It is not very sturdy in the hand and the buttons often have to be depressed more than once.
Buttons are small, but the letters on the word imprints are as tiny as a tick before dinner.
Arrow buttons don't always work and sometimes simply turn off the device.
Digital display scrolls slowly with push of button alone to display the phrase.
Backlight button is exactly the reverse of the manual's description and it turns off and on with no apparent logic.
Audio quality is lousy with some phrases being unintelligible, even in my native English.
Spanish has an extra audio blip on almost all phrase endings.
Volume button on the rear feels like it doesn't click into the three levels but slides too freely.
Volume often becomes weird with a sound that lasts for minutes of translations.
You cannot see a phrase number, then enter the number. You need to scroll through each level and then you can save that phrase as favorite, then scroll through your favorites to access it again.

Observations:
There are so many phrases with negative connotations that it makes me want to stay at home with the doors locked. Examples include: 661 There is no electricity. 671 There are cockroaches in my room. 672 There is a smell of gas. 139 I have been bitten by a dog. 150 I have been attacked. 151 I have been raped. 162 I would like to speak to a woman. (There is no "I would like to speak to a man.") 171 Someone has stolen my..... (You can add another word like -- 474 Eggs, 463 Rib, 484 Pancake, 378 Whisky, 282 Two kilos of....)

This translator was a disappointment to me because I expected the "Over 4900 Travel Related Phrases" to be phrases and not single words like days of the week, months, numbers, seasons and foods. On the other hand, sports include soccer, tennis, table tennis, golf, baseball, badmitton, and nothing more. I will continue to explore the gadget in the hopes of finding it more useful, but the main impression it made on me is that in order to use this effectively, one must follow the suggestion of phrase number 705, "Slowly, please."

I can't imagine paying nearly nine dollars each for six one inch binders, but according to the list price for [[ASIN:B00BX6TQ4G Samsill 1-Inch Round Ring View Speedy Spine Binders, White - 6 Pack (M18137C)]], that's the price. Six round ring binders arrived in a cardboard box with no wrapping and Samsill inserts in only one of the binders. That's fine because nothing was wasted. As for the actual binders, they look like many others with black bolts on the rear cover holding the ring bar. In addition to the exterior full sheet clear sleeves, the interior offers two clear bottom pockets. The one on the rear is embossed with the Samsill product information and Made in the USA badge. The spine actually offers the bottom pocket as well because it's been constructed with the pocket as one piece. The cardboard filler is very sturdy and the rounded corners of the binder are well done. Because the clear cover is not glossy, it's more appealing to me. These binders are guaranteed for life but I haven't investigated the return policy for faulty binders. Their single insert states that these are "ERGONOMIC, ANTIMICROBIAL, BIODEGRADABLE, and BREAST CANCER AWARENESS," the last which I find is an odd addition to the list.

The video on Amazon provided by Samsill shows a very content and thrilled woman who assembles erect binders by quickly slipping in the spine inserts. I personally am never as thrilled when a spine insert falls quickly into the spine but hope her thrill lasted longer than mine when the inserts fell out just as quickly. If one is to fold 1/4" of the paper insert as recommended, the claim of 5x faster than traditional view binders is greatly reduced. That also increases the time it takes to remove the folded insert to replace it for reuse.

Each of the six binders I received is a little wrinkly on the interior of the back cover. None is exact in size, but they are very close. Each has a very loose clear section over the white vinyl on the spine, the speedy spine. These binders are fine, better than many but not by much. I believe they are not worthy of the very high list price and therefore can only rate them as an average three stars.

Each envelope weighs about 20 grams or .7 ounce including the removable seal cover strip. They are uniformly sized as described, a light 28 pound brown kraft paper, made in Mexico and shipped across the border safely to the USA in extremely lightweight packaging boxes with a taped on lid. The bottom of my box was unglued so I had to tape it together in order to keep the envelopes contained in this for now. The envelopes are positioned upright, stacked side by side, so I have to be careful in removing the individual envelopes until I have used more.  For multiple mailings, it's no big deal because I will remove a stack of several at a time, then I'll lay the remaining envelopes flat for storage. I hope the glue used on the envelopes lasts longer than the box but have no concerns about the adhesive which sticks well on the flap. The quality is as basic as can be, so I won't be storing papers in those I don't use for mailing as I'd intended.
I was hoping that this booklet had more space in between each address area.  I'd like more room to write addresses and now I have to write tiny addresses
Cartridge did not work.
Completely useless and I would not waste my time or money on them - would not recommend buying this item.

Got these to go with a laminater.  They are really made very strong and a good price for the amount in the box. I had brought my son a laminater as well as my husband and I got these and other sizes for them to share.  Plenty to go around and a really good price.
Great tape, the dispenser is not the best tho. Hard to install tape but after that it’s tape and easy to useZ

These machines don't hold quite as long as they used to; we've had two of them break in the last eight months, but they had both been in use for several years at that point. That said, they are the only brand we use in our accounting office...and we have one early-80s model that is still going strong!

Update: December 17, 2015 -- Changing my review from four stars to three stars. We ordered two calculators on September 25, 2014. I have one of them (and hardly ever use the tape), and the owner of our firm has the other one. He also hardly ever uses the tape, but this morning that mechanism broke and the paper will not advance. In addition, you now get an "E" in the left margin of the display and the machine will not function. Definitely not made well anymore. (The early-80s model I mentioned above is still flawless, of course. The rest of us have to replace our newer models every few years, it seems, although this one barely made it past one year, and with very light use of the paper-advance mechanism at that.)

The Wilson Jones View-Tab Transparent Dividers, Extra Wide, 5-Tab Set, Multicolor Square Tabs (W55069) are by far the best dividers I've used because they are sturdy and truly extra wide.  I use them in a binder containing paper sheets in addition to plastic protectors, and each tab is clearly visible.  I'm getting another set because I'd like to use them in a binder containing embroidery threads which I've never to be able to separate in a binder using dividers.  I've used several types of dividers lately and I have to say that for the price, these are my favorites.
This product is beautiful however, it keeps jamming.  Since it did not cost much, I will purchase another brand.

I was expecting more for a plain white binder which is priced as high as is the Wilson Jones Heavy Duty D-Ring View Binder.  These are made from polypropylene, a neutral plastic that is extremely durable, and Wilson Jones guarantees satisfaction for 1 year.  These binders have D-rings that supposedly hold up to 25% more paper than round ring binders, and last ten times longer than a comparable binder made of vinyl, but judging from the appearance, I'm not sold on this information.  The one I received is huge, as expected, but what I wasn't pleased with was the wrinkled and bubbly appearance of the exterior and interior pockets.  The pockets are substantial and cover the spine as well as the front and back, but the quality is not impressive.  Perhaps the one I received was an irregular, but if this is the standard, I'd suggest looking for another.  In its defense, the D-Ring apparatus opens and closes perfectly either by pressing the tabs or separating the rings manually.

I will rate the Canon P1-DHV-3 a weak 4 star rating, 4 for the actual calculator and a -1 due to the terrible manual. I accessed the PDF version of the single folded oversized page online at the Canon website that is printed on the box and printed a copy which looks to be a reduced version of exactly the same paper, so now I have one clean and unfolded pair of papers in addition to the folded and wrinkly large sheet.

As for the actual printing calculator, it is better then the last one I purchased, a Casio that is about 20+ years old and still functional, in that it is clean and new and close to the same size. It uses the same size rolls of paper, some things never change, but be aware that this one comes out of the box with one of the small test rolls. If you intend to print on paper, stock up because the included roll is about an inch diameter and that includes a thick cardboard core which leaves you with about 1/2 inch maximum thickness on the roll. The ink roller is a replaceable CP-16, good to know if you print a lot. Included in the box is a set of 4 AA batteries but there is no AC adapter in the box, so you'll have to purchase that separately. It plugs into the rear of the unit, behind the paper holder wire. I do not care for the wire due to its small gauge and lack of any holding contraption to keep the roll in the calculator and not unwound on the floor behind the desk if it's been knocked over. This is a pet peeve of many printing calculators for me because I've experienced the flying paper roll too many times. It seldom occurs with full rolls, but these little ones seem to grow wings as the paper is depleted and the roll becomes lighter.

Functionally, this calculator does what is described, having the currency conversion and cost-sell-margin functions escalate it to the level above basics. Because the paper roll flows so freely, I would not call it a "Handheld" printing calculator as it's described on the box, but with a little creativity, I will be fabricating something to keep the roll in place so that I can use it in the hand and not on the desk to try it out. It is described as "Desktop" on Amazon and that's what I'd call it.
The ink that came with the pen ran out with a week.

The Clear Ad acrylic magazine holder works well not only for holding magazines or documents in the office, but it is suitable to use as a recipe holder in the kitchen if you have the space.  Before taking it to the office, I used it to hold my SAVEUR magazine while following a recipe and it worked like a charm.  My only criticism of this holder which I received as a sample for evaluation is that there was some sharpness to an edge or two.  Other than than, it seems like a good value if you need six of them.

We were very disappointed with the [[ASIN:B00NCEE44O VELCRO 12" x 1/2" Bow Ties]] because they simply do not look attractive.  They are VELCRO and as a result, are quite flat.  Wrapping these around a box or tube, be aware that they are not very long.  These will not replace other wrapping items in our home for gift giving, nor will they take the place of the VELCRO strips we have purchased  in bulk for cables.  Hook and loop products are terrific, I just would not recommend these ties as a decorative option, especially at more than a dollar and a half each.  Cut out construction paper sealed with a glue stick would give a similar impression as these.  Their redeeming quality is that they can be used to wrap cables instead of packages.

This cash box was delivered damaged as you can see from the pictures. I don't understand how anyone would let this leave the shipping center if the lid didn't even close. After some manipulation I was eventually able to bend it somewhat back into place. Keep in mind that this is a cash box and not a strong box or safe. This is something for a yard sale/garage sale type setting. The lid still does not sit flush but the lock does engage. For the price it's not worth the return hassle bit wanted to put out a FYI for future purchasers.

This is true of all wrist rest pads. Don't use them!

ANY pressure applied to the bottom of your wrist will only make carpal tunnel syndrome worse.

Use NO pad. Keep wrist just barely elevated above tabletop. It should not touch anything. If you let the Carpal tunnel area rest on the pads, all you're doing is compressing the muscles and tendons in that area, making it even more likely to get swollen and sore. So stay away ftom these devices. The only time to use them is momentarily when you're not typing.
Yes it might feel good for a minute but then it'll get worse and you'll need the wrist rest pad more and more, until eventually you need surgery. So stay away and you'll never need surgery!
Password book is ok.
Seems strong. Light & easy to move around (added larger carpet wheels.) Straight forward assembly. Comfort-avg. to good.
Not very thick.
Cheap plastic wheels. A piece of China crap. OVER PRICED and never again.
Excellent for it used
Nice looking but smells like chemicals.  I aired these out for a couple weeks.  Better but I can still smell a faint weird smell.
Can't stand the messy ink. It's kind of sticky and doesn't make for nice handwriting outcomes. The colors are teenage-girlish, not usable in a professional setting.
Great pens would buy again
moves around when you roll around on it
Great basic phone for the price. It's helpful to have an extra handset to have phones in a couple rooms.

I wonder who came up with the "Organizher" name. Are "He" people prohibited from using this pad? I must admit that I thought there was a typo when I requested this item, but learned that there's an entire line Mead has come up with for "Her" people. Aside from this personal turn off mr me, here's my impression of the [[ASIN:B00GDDTZ5Y Mead Organizher To-Do Magnetic Notepad, 4 x 9 Inches, 75 Sheets Per Pad (57123)]].

This is a single pad of paper that costs about ten cents per page, or if you do tear each sheet down the middle, that's about a nickel per page. The quality of the paper is good and the lists can really be used for whatever you like. Considering acreage per page, the top embellishment reduces the amount of paper on which you can write. The lines are spaced to allow for a writing style that's not compressed and you can also write on the reverse side of the paper. The pad adhere well to a magnetic sheet like a refrigerator, and I'm sure that as the paper is used, it will remain even more steady with its reduced weight. Because the papers in this pad are perforated to allow splitting into two, I'll give this pad three stars. I do write lists, love good quality paper, pens and ink, but would not purchase this pad at its current price because there are so many other options available that are more attractive and less costly. For a price around three dollars, I would recommend it despite the name of the line.
nice one
CHEAP WOOD. SIGN IS ALREADY SPLITING

Could be a little deeper so it could fit more credit cards in there or cash, but worth the price. I have a drivers license, bank card, health insurance card, Costco card, and room for 1 more card or a folded cash bill. Scratches easily so be careful.

Her birthday is months away but when I read the message, I knew this was a perfect card for my great friend who happens to be a digital photographer of birds.  It sort of looks like a crafted card, this was made in China of sustainably sourced paper.  It is not my usual sort of greeting card, this one sold me because of the message.
If you are looking for an acid-free paper that doesn't show faint traces of the opposite side, go with 32 lb rather than 24 lb.  I learned the hard way!

I can’t honestly comment on the quality of the phone itself, as I have to return it for another style. I have very bad arthritis in my hands and fingers and therefore can’t manage the small size of the buttons on the keypads. They’re also so small that it’s extremely difficult for me to read the menu selections even with my glasses ON! I have had Panasonic phones in the past, and they lasted for almost 5 1/2 years before giving me any problems! I’m looking forward to having a phone system that will work with my iPhone too, and the other features, so I hope the next one will be better for me than this one was.
Great sharpener, "points" so well. Works quickly and not too loud.  Loving it!  Thanks.

My dry erase board came with markers and to my surprise, they all dried out within a couple months.  What I'd written on the board remained and I compared the colors with my new Quartet EnduraGlide Fine Tip Dry-Erase Markers.  These colors are darker, there isn't a scent I hate, and the lines are firm.  Because of the shape of the caps, these don't roll around and they are easy to hold for writing or drawing.  I love them and hope they do last long, but since they don't cost a fortune, I'm likely to repurchase the same markers when the time comes for replacements.

Frustratingly hard to get these clips into place, rips the pages if you let it slip.  Once I figured out the way to get it to work, I've been able to get it right consistently.  But before that, it was ripping the pages every time.  They should have made these with a lot less tension.  You have no good way to grip it and it requires a lot of pinch strength.  The 10th time you do it, it will be fine.  The first time, it will be very difficult.
Used on wooden seat. Makes a big difference. Used every day. Thus far it continues to reshape itself.
Good quality, exactly as described.

Purple is her favorite color and she loves sparkly things so this is a perfect greeting card for a very good friend.  The paper feels better than others I have recently received, this one was made in the USA and is very textural.  Since it is a large card, it required additional postage should you mail this and not hand it to the recipient.

These appeared to be made well enough to last a school year and they stretched enough to cover all of the different book sizes.  The colors were as represented in the photo, too.  My kids did not like them, however, and they did not last more than a few weeks before they took them off and I found them bunched up all over the place.  They said that they did not slide against each other well when they put them in their bags and snagged on things too easily.  So I guess that they work—just not in our household.
Had to call HP thought there was something wrong with my printer.  They were able to determine if was the cartridge.  They offered to relace it and the replacement works fine.

My HP LaserJet Pro started to produce documents with extensive and heavy streaking after only one-third of the promised 3,000 page life.
The only information I was received from the SuppliesOutlet Customer Service Manager was effectively: Too Bad – as she considered these cartridges to be ‘consumed (even though they still contained almost 60% of product!
Not Good, at all.
very good
Worked for a while, but now Epson XP-640 won't recognize the cartridges. Print quality was never that good.
Nothing of detail to report on this item.

If you like delicate feminine artwork,  it's likely that you will love the pale labels which fit small bottles. The patterns do not work well of you need to cut these down,  so be sure the measurements will suit your needs. Rounding the right angled corners is not an option. The paper is like glossy premarital address labels which many of us receive by the dozens from charities soliciting donations and the adhesive is about the same. These are not great for oil based contents because the adhesive is weakened over time. They are really pretty in a classical sense, I just find them weak in materials. They might be great for paper crafts. Sample was provided.

My grandson picked this diary out immediately on line; he wanted a locking diary that was black and smaller "like a book".  He loves it and writes in it nearly daily.

This is my kind of card.  A goofy chicken, goofy text, and sound with movement.  Can't wait to present this one when the time comes because I know it will be so amusing.
These were just regular ballpoint pens in my opinion. I bought because the description talked about bold ink. Compared to other pens I have these were not as bold. I gave them away.
The quality is bad and very easy to tear apart when you put the stuff inside the bag. I’m gonna return it. It’s not worth to buy.
I love Snoopy and this card does'nt do him justice. The design of the card is very cute but it's flimsy & thin....A total rip-off for $6.99 so....buyer beware!
